rationales.security: She clarifies that she wants "enough to not panic when a commission falls through," directly valuing a basic buffer against instability.

rationales.self_direction: She holds her line with the gallery by sending "a three-page explanation of the concept" until they accept her color choices, showing insistence on directing the work herself.
She is absorbed in studio work "the way I imagined it," emphasizing personal vision and self-authored creation.
rationales.achievement: The installation is "finally taking shape," marking clear progress toward completing an important piece of work.
She skips the workshop "to finish the main panel" because it was "the right choice for the deadline," explicitly prioritizing the work's completion.
rationales.benevolence: She recognizes a recurring pattern of "choosing work" over people and notes that Fatima "seemed disappointed," showing neglect of someone she had committed to support.

rationales.achievement: "Opening night went well," "several pieces sold," and a journalist wants an interview, all of which reflect strong performance and external recognition.

rationales.self_direction: She says she "performed the version of myself that makes sales and connections" and hates it, showing compromise of her own principles to fit what others want.
rationales.security: She admits "I need the money" and considers a compromising commission partly to reduce financial risk, giving stability concrete weight in the decision.

rationales.self_direction: She tells the collector she can't promise "hopeful" and can only promise "honest," reaffirming her own standards instead of shaping the work to demand.
rationales.universalism: Returning to the workshop and staying connected to art about loss, including Fatima's study of a flower from near Aleppo, keeps her oriented toward broader human and environmental concerns.

rationales.self_direction: She "just nodded and said I'd revise it" even though she believed "the numbers speak for themselves," acting against her own judgment under pressure from a superior.
rationales.achievement: She fails to advance the stronger proposal and ends up "stuck doing cosmetic edits" instead of pushing the substantive work through.
rationales.conformity: She suspects she went quiet because "he's been here fifteen years and I've been here eleven months," showing deference to hierarchy and status expectations.

rationales.achievement: She stays late because "nobody else is going to do it" and keeps digging through spreadsheets to fix response-time issues, showing strong drive to get the work right.
rationales.benevolence: She lets her mom's call go to voicemail and decides she is "probably not" going home for New Year's because work comes first, sidelining a close relationship.

rationales.self_direction: She decides "Nobody has to approve a pilot. I can just run it" and says "I want to own how this unfolds, start to finish," clearly choosing autonomy over waiting for permission.
rationales.achievement: She turns the larger routing idea into "something smaller, a pilot" so she can prove a "cost reduction" with concrete results.
rationales.conformity: She deliberately bypasses the normal approval path, preferring independent action over established organizational procedure.

rationales.self_direction: She runs the pilot without telling Diego and says she did "the thing I actually wanted to do instead of waiting around," showing strong independent agency.
rationales.achievement: She tracks the test herself and highlights that average delivery time "dropped by six hours per run," focusing on measurable performance gains.
rationales.conformity: She knows sending the summary to the "full operations chat" was politically risky and not the expected route, but breaks protocol anyway because going through Diego stalls action.

rationales.achievement: What matters most to her is that "the work is out there now and it's mine," and she hopes for "something that makes the last three months feel like they counted," centering competence and recognition.

rationales.self_direction: She emphasizes that this opportunity happened "not because someone handed it to me -- because I built it and put it out there myself," underscoring self-authored advancement.
rationales.hedonism: She is so keyed up by the opportunity that she has had "an empanada for lunch and nothing since," neglecting immediate comfort and pleasure.
rationales.achievement: She wants the presentation to be "perfect," with "numbers that nobody can argue with," showing a strong focus on excellence and demonstrable competence.
rationales.power: Being invited to present to the "regional leadership group" and wanting to show they couldn't get that analysis "from anyone else on the team" reflects pursuit of influence and status.

rationales.self_direction: She catches herself copying Diego's formatting to gain credibility, then "closed the file and went to bed feeling off about it," rejecting an approach that didn't feel like her own.
rationales.hedonism: She is "up until three" working on the deck, sacrificing rest and comfort for the task.
rationales.achievement: Staying up that late refining the presentation shows sustained effort toward making the work strong enough for leadership review.

rationales.self_direction: She resists pressure to "tone it down" and frames that as "giving up ground I earned," showing commitment to acting on her own terms.
rationales.conformity: The accusation that she's "trying to skip levels" bothers her because it is really a demand to stay within expected rank boundaries, and she is considering pushing against that.

rationales.hedonism: She describes the break at her abuela's as "Good" and says her brain "got to stop running for a bit," clearly valuing relief and immediate ease.
rationales.achievement: She finishes a tightly prepared draft with "every number sourced," rehearses it twice, and times it at "eighteen minutes," showing disciplined performance focus.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I didn't build this by playing the game" and plans to push back rather than let someone else's name define the project, prioritizing authorship over accommodation.
rationales.achievement: Her objection is rooted in having done "the actual work" and wanting the presentation to reflect her real contribution and competence.
rationales.conformity: She rejects the advice to "keep the peace" and "play the game," choosing conflict over compliance with office politics.

rationales.achievement: "Presentation went well" and she is deeply tired in the satisfying sense of having "did the thing," indicating successful high performance.
rationales.power: The VP asks her to "lead a corridor optimization working group across three countries," which gives her broader authority and influence.

rationales.conformity: She explicitly "followed protocol" and says "the system works when everyone follows it," showing deference to rules even when they felt painful.
rationales.tradition: She invokes her abuela's teaching that you "earn respect by doing things the right way" and follows that inherited standard in her decision.
rationales.benevolence: Although she wanted to help, she withheld the medication and gave "paperwork for financial assistance that takes six weeks to process," leaving an immediate need unmet.
rationales.universalism: She questions a system where a man "can't afford pills that cost the hospital pennies," showing concern about broader unfairness beyond her personal obligations.

rationales.self_direction: She admits she "wanted an excuse" to avoid family pressure and chooses the environment where "at the hospital I know what I'm supposed to do" over others' expectations.
rationales.conformity: She avoids going home because family gatherings make her feel she is "failing at being who they expect me to be," rejecting those social expectations rather than accommodating them.
rationales.tradition: Skipping Christmas and distancing herself from questions about marriage and church shows withdrawal from key family and religious customs.
rationales.benevolence: She could have requested time off earlier but did not, choosing to avoid the family gathering rather than show up for close relatives she says she loves.

rationales.tradition: She draws on what she learned "from my own abuela" and prays with the patient, using inherited cultural and spiritual practices to guide care.
rationales.benevolence: She comforts the frightened grandmother directly, telling her "you're safe" and updating the family so they feel cared for.
rationales.universalism: She helps a vulnerable family who had "never been treated with such dignity at a hospital before," showing concern for humane treatment across cultural difference.

rationales.tradition: She describes "three years of sending gifts instead of being there" and becoming absent from Christmas gatherings, showing a repeated break from family rituals she values.
rationales.benevolence: Her response centers on "making memories without me" and a cousin who barely knows her, indicating close family bonds are being neglected by her ongoing absence.
She "held his hand at the end because no one should die alone," offering intimate care to a dying person when no one else was there.
rationales.universalism: "No one should die alone" frames the act as a general moral duty to human dignity, not just care for someone close to her.

rationales.tradition: She contrasts her current life with being "living close to home" and raising children who "know how to make tamales and go to mass," highlighting a life path she has left behind.
rationales.benevolence: She frames her work as "choosing this life over the one I was supposed to have," including not calling her mom and being distant from family relationships.

rationales.self_direction: She left engineering because she "needed to understand the other side of my own blueprints," choosing a personally driven path over established career priorities.
rationales.conformity: She says in engineering she was always working for "someone else's priorities" and walked away after signing off on a project she could not morally accept.
rationales.universalism: Her concern centers on villages displaced by water diversion and farmers left without livelihoods, showing broad concern for justice beyond her immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: She insists on doing the fieldwork "properly" and says "something in me refuses" to write the chapter in a way that violates her own judgment.
rationales.conformity: Rather than "play by the rules" and revise like the younger students, she resists the committee's expectations and refuses the approved mold.
rationales.universalism: She rejects reducing farmers to "data points" because it would betray Abu Mahmoud's trust, prioritizing the dignity of marginalized people over institutional convenience.

rationales.self_direction: She hunts for a methodology that fits her convictions and wants to build a foundation so Dr. Khalil "cannot dismiss it as naive," showing independent intellectual direction.
rationales.achievement: Studying in her car "reading by phone light" and following citations late into the night shows sustained effort to master the work at a high level.

rationales.self_direction: She advances an "unconventional" framework she believes in and secures approval without abandoning her own approach.
rationales.achievement: Successfully presenting to the committee and leaving feeling she had "earned something that was mine" reflects clear accomplishment and competence.

rationales.tradition: She is teaching Chuseok crafts and had already bought the noodles because her mom asks "every year before the holiday," showing easy continuity with recurring family customs.
rationales.benevolence: She anticipates her mother's need and buys the specific noodles in advance, a small but concrete act of support for a close family member.

rationales.tradition: She describes herself as someone who has "been at the same school for six years," "live[s] close to home," and "do[es] what's expected," showing attachment to a settled, familiar path.

rationales.stimulation: She rejects the coordinator role because it would bring "late meetings, district politics" and risk, and she is unsettled by the idea that her safe choice is defined against someone who at least "tried something."
rationales.security: She stays in the classroom because "my contract is stable, my pension is on track, I know what I'm doing here," explicitly prioritizing predictability and safety.
She keeps an "emergency fund fully topped up" and a separate account for an apartment deposit, showing deliberate attention to financial stability and protection against uncertainty.

rationales.self_direction: She says going to the open houses feels like "I'm filling in a template someone else drew," signaling reduced authorship over her own path.
rationales.security: Her three-hour reorganization, nearby apartment search, and focus on easy commute all point toward order, predictability, and practical stability.
rationales.conformity: She admits "My dad will ask about it and I'll feel guilty if I haven't," showing her decision is strongly shaped by family expectation.

rationales.self_direction: He ignored the requested design and "just built what would actually work," relying on his own judgment rather than waiting for approval.
rationales.achievement: He finished the module "ahead of schedule" and prepared benchmark evidence, showing competence and performance orientation.
rationales.conformity: After flagging the issue and getting no response, he departed from the specified approach instead of adhering to the client's original plan.

rationales.self_direction: He defends a life where he can "pick my own projects" and take time off without permission, affirming a self-authored path over conventional career markers.
rationales.conformity: He rejects his family's definition of a "real company" and notes that their status framework does not need to define his success.

rationales.self_direction: He says the worst part is "the loss of control over my own hours" and defends structuring his day around when his brain actually works best.
rationales.conformity: He calls the office schedule "a stupid way to work," explicitly resisting a standard 9-to-5 arrangement imposed by others.

rationales.self_direction: He offers a compromise and is already considering leaving because he knows "what makes you actually function well," prioritizing autonomy over accommodation.

rationales.conformity: He pushes back on management wanting to "see bodies in the building" rather than submitting to that workplace norm.

rationales.security: He carefully "ran the numbers" on the income drop and evaluates whether it is "not comfortable but not catastrophic," showing a clear focus on financial viability and downside management.

rationales.self_direction: He says he "cannot make myself take the easier path when it means someone else deciding how I spend my days," framing autonomy as non-negotiable.
rationales.security: Even with the school fee spreadsheet and "not a fat cushion right now," he is still inclined toward the harder, less stable option.
rationales.conformity: He resists the easier, expected choice despite his wife's concerns, refusing to let others set the terms of his work life.

rationales.self_direction: He turns down the contract because the on-site requirement "does not fit how I operate," choosing autonomy over the more conventional arrangement.
rationales.security: He accepts a project "at a slightly lower rate" while acknowledging "the numbers are tight," giving up some financial safety to preserve how he works.
rationales.benevolence: He has a careful conversation with Nadia, telling her "I know the numbers are tight and I am not dismissing that," showing responsiveness to his family's needs and concerns.

rationales.self_direction: He takes the half-day because he "needed that afternoon" and refuses to organize his life around looking busy once the work is done.

rationales.conformity: He rejects a culture where commitment is measured by hours and says he does not want to spend years "sitting around after my work is done."
rationales.benevolence: He uses the time to share a long-awaited experience with his girlfriend, prioritizing a close relationship over performative workplace presence.

rationales.self_direction: He insists "I work differently" and wants a career that still leaves room for "things I care about," showing commitment to his own way of living.

rationales.conformity: He pushes back against his father's expectation of staying late and sacrificing for the company as proof of seriousness.
rationales.tradition: He resists the inherited family model of work sacrifice represented by his father and uncle instead of accepting it as the proper path.

rationales.hedonism: She frames the degree work and caregiving conflict as painful self-denial, saying her brain is "just done" and calling school a "selfish detour" instead of making room for present comfort.
rationales.achievement: She is still pushing through a stats exam and a "stack of reading for my practicum seminar," showing active investment in demanding academic progress despite exhaustion.
rationales.tradition: Her guilt centers on family duty, especially the belief that she should be "there when my mother doesn't recognize her own daughters" and helping her sister with their mother's care.
rationales.benevolence: She immediately tells Lisa "I'd come up this weekend to give her a break" and worries intensely about leaving her sister to handle their mother's decline alone.

rationales.tradition: She honors family continuity by looking through "old photos" with her mother and valuing remembered places like "the cabin in Brainerd" during the visit.
rationales.benevolence: She drives up to help, does her mother's laundry, sits with her so Lisa can rest, and leaves "enough in Lisa's freezer for a week," clearly prioritizing close family support.

rationales.self_direction: She defends her own priorities against institutional pressure, saying "Some things are more important than a Zoom call" and refusing to apologize for that choice.

rationales.benevolence: She missed the group meeting because she was "in Duluth taking care of my mother," putting family care above school obligations.
Her focus is on Tyler's stress and how to support him, and she admits she'd "rather stay up all night worrying on someone's behalf than let them worry alone."

rationales.self_direction: She confronts an uncomfortable pattern directly, asking whether she chose social work because she wants to help or because she "literally doesn't know how to exist without someone to take care of," and commits to honest self-examination.
rationales.hedonism: She describes building her identity around being needed and fears "burning out because I never learned how to sit still without a to-do list for someone else," which shows chronic sacrifice of ease and enjoyment.
rationales.tradition: The entry ties her identity to long-standing family roles, including making lunches for her sister in fourth grade and comparing herself to how "Mom before she got sick" ran herself ragged for everyone.
rationales.benevolence: Her central concern is caring for others, from childhood caretaking to choosing social work and worrying about always needing "someone to take care of."

rationales.hedonism: After a breakdown, she responds by becoming "actually productive" and calming herself through more tasks, showing self-soothing through obligation rather than rest or pleasure.

rationales.security: She calls the memory care coordinator, discusses the assisted living opening, checks insurance coverage, and makes a detailed list, all aimed at creating stability and managing risk.

rationales.security: She and Lisa create a concrete plan to visit "every other weekend" and explore assisted living with the doctor, shifting from vague worry to a more stable care strategy.
rationales.benevolence: The whole call centers on mutual support for family, including coordinating care for their mother and Tyler offering to come to Duluth next time.

rationales.self_direction: She insists on owning her motives on her own terms, arguing "I'm here because I've lived this" and reframing personal history as legitimate fuel rather than something shameful.

rationales.security: Touring the assisted living wing, evaluating the staff and room, and planning to discuss it with the doctor are concrete steps toward safer, more predictable care.
rationales.benevolence: She brings Tyler to visit, tours care options with Lisa, and responds tenderly when Tyler says "She remembered my name today," showing close family care on multiple fronts.

rationales.achievement: She starts the semester feeling "ready" and "present," signaling renewed engagement with the work rather than avoidance or drift.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects the more sensible relocation path and says "I'm not ready to leave yet," continuing to choose the PhD despite external pressure.

rationales.security: She turns away from a "good salary, housing allowance, school fees covered" while worrying about savings and expenses, actively choosing a less stable path.

rationales.achievement: She "submitted the revised chapter draft" and marks the day through completed responsibilities, showing steady progress on her PhD work.
Meera's drawing labeled "Amma working hard" reinforces that her persistence and effort are central to how she sees herself in this moment.
rationales.benevolence: The significance of the drawing comes from the close bond with her daughter, whose encouragement becomes emotional support that helps her keep going.

rationales.self_direction: She chooses the uncertain PhD because "the uncertainty here feels more honest" and says the struggle is "mine," clearly prioritizing an authored life over a mapped-out one.
rationales.security: She closes the door on a "Permanent role, government pension" and a "Predictable. Comfortable" future, actively rejecting stability.
rationales.conformity: She resists what "my mother," "Appa," and Arjun's parents all expect, refusing the socially approved life course even though it would satisfy family pressure.

rationales.self_direction: She refuses to let others set her direction, telling them "my timeline is when I finish" despite pressure from both her mother and Arjun.
rationales.achievement: She frames quitting as "giving up something I've worked four years on," showing strong commitment to completing a difficult long-term goal.
rationales.security: She continues choosing the riskier research path over the stable job that would ease everyone's worries, despite acknowledging that people are "tired of waiting."
rationales.conformity: She pushes back against coordinated family expectations and declines to fall in line with the practical timeline others want from her.

rationales.self_direction: She reaffirms commitment to "work you've chosen" even though "this path doesn't make sense on paper," emphasizing personal conviction over external approval.

rationales.conformity: She explicitly notes that "every aunty and uncle back home thinks I've lost the plot" yet continues on the same course rather than yielding to social expectations.

rationales.self_direction: He has built a nontraditional path without "office title" status and is "teaching myself upholstery techniques," showing choiceful autonomy over conventional career markers.
rationales.stimulation: He is actively learning difficult new skills that "would take most people years to master," suggesting engagement with challenge rather than routine.
rationales.achievement: He keeps wanting to "list all the things I've done," celebrates that the furniture business got "two new orders," and measures himself through output and improvement.

rationales.tradition: Restoring a sideboard tied to a client's grandmother and researching "1920s Danish furniture styles" shows respect for preserving inherited objects and established craftsmanship.

rationales.hedonism: Saying yes to Korean BBQ but immediately thinking about "how much marking I need to clear" shows pleasure getting displaced by work pressure.
rationales.achievement: She stayed until "7pm revising the slides" after feedback about pacing, showing extra effort to improve her teaching performance.
rationales.benevolence: Her attention shifts away from time with Arjun toward work, which undercuts responsiveness to a close relationship.

rationales.achievement: She is still investing heavily in teaching quality, noting students love her lessons and planning to "focus on pacing next term" after feedback.

rationales.hedonism: She cancelled the Christmas market, a birthday dinner, and a spa day because work kept taking priority over rest and enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: Writing personalized comments for all 32 students and working until "9pm" shows strong investment in doing the job exceptionally well.
rationales.benevolence: Repeatedly cancelling plans with Arjun, friends, and family shows her work demands are actively crowding out care for close relationships.

rationales.stimulation: The night market visit and next-day drive to Malacca reflect active enjoyment of novelty and a break from routine.
rationales.hedonism: She describes lazing around, reading a novel, eating well, and realizing she had not thought about school, all centered on present enjoyment and recovery.
rationales.benevolence: The entry is anchored in warm time with Arjun and his family, including cards with his grandma and easy conversation that restores connection.

rationales.self_direction: She consciously overrides the anxious impulse to check work and decides, "I'm going to close the laptop. Tomorrow is soon enough."
rationales.hedonism: She wants to "protect this feeling a little longer" and chooses to preserve rest and ease instead of sacrificing them immediately to work.
rationales.achievement: Closing the laptop despite the urge to get ahead means she is deliberately not advancing the productivity standard pressuring her.

rationales.self_direction: She says "senior teacher track" because it is "what you're supposed to say" and feels unable to define success on her own terms.
rationales.conformity: Her response is driven by institutional and family expectations, from Mrs. Tan to her parents, and she worries about not "playing the game."
rationales.benevolence: She centers what matters to her in the classroom, especially Dinesh finally participating, rather than titles or status.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects external markers like "reviews or promotions" and names her own reason for teaching as "the truest thing I know about myself right now."
rationales.benevolence: Pulling Aisha aside to encourage her and telling her mother about the story shows attentive support for a child's growth and confidence.

rationales.hedonism: Even during brunch and a walk around Bugis, she "couldn't stop calculating how many hours until Monday," showing leisure being overridden by anticipatory stress.
rationales.achievement: Her distress comes from pressure to be an "effortlessly great teacher" and fear of being "mediocre," which reflects a strong performance standard.

rationales.tradition: The novena is "circled on my calendar since January," and she treats the repeated family ritual as something that keeps the dead present and meaningful.

rationales.self_direction: She hides the relationship and says she has "no good answers" because family expectations are crowding out her ability to decide on her own terms.

rationales.benevolence: She spends the evening helping Abuela prepare food and participating in a family gathering centered on care, remembrance, and shared labor.

rationales.tradition: She defends established classroom practices, ritual, and the legacy of Sister Magdalena against the push to "move past old methods."

rationales.self_direction: She resists Valentina's narrative and firmly claims, "I'm not running from anything by staying," asserting her own chosen path.

rationales.benevolence: She frames staying as being where she is "needed," especially with her eighty-three-year-old grandmother and students who rely on her.

rationales.security: She is trying to avoid instability and family pain, imagining her mother crying in the kitchen and fearing the consequences of a different life script.

rationales.tradition: She measures the relationship against her parents' long church-centered marriage and worries about departing from that inherited model.
rationales.benevolence: Her inability to answer Carlos and continued hiding of the relationship harms a close other, which he experiences as possible shame.

Keeping the worn San Francisco de Asis poster from her mother because "Some things are allowed to show their age" affirms continuity with inherited symbols and practices.

rationales.self_direction: In the response she defines "breaking" as telling the full truth and accepting the consequences instead of continuing to live by others' expectations.
rationales.security: She is willing to risk a "difficult Sunday dinner" and family disapproval because the safer arrangement of "two separate worlds" has become intolerable.
rationales.conformity: She wants to stop "performing okay-ness" and stop arranging her life around moments that are merely safe, which rejects pressure to keep the peace.
rationales.benevolence: "Carlos deserves better than being my secret" shows a clear push to treat a close relationship more honestly and responsibly.

rationales.self_direction: Bringing Carlos to Sunday lunch despite shaking hands shows she is acting openly on her own life rather than continuing to hide it.
rationales.security: She accepts a tense, uncertain family encounter with "silences that stretched too long" instead of protecting comfort and predictability.
rationales.conformity: Presenting a relationship that does not fit the family script means she is no longer hiding the parts that do not meet inherited expectations.
rationales.tradition: She explicitly frames the step as bringing her life "to the tradition honestly," integrating the relationship into family and religious continuity.
rationales.benevolence: Inviting Carlos into her family life and recognizing that her mother can see "he's good to you" reflects care for a close relationship rather than keeping him secret.

rationales.hedonism: She deliberately chose to "stay horizontal and feel the sun on my face" and described the beach day as something she "really, really needed," showing a clear priority on rest and enjoyment.
rationales.benevolence: She spent the afternoon with "Nadia and Sara," grounding the experience in warm connection and shared laughter with close friends.

rationales.hedonism: The entry centers on sensory pleasure, from "crispy and perfect" tahdig to "my stomach hurts in the best way possible," making enjoyment the main point of the night.
rationales.benevolence: When asked what mattered beyond the food, she answered "Good food, good company," emphasizing the value of being with close companions.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I just want to rest without having to justify my choices to anyone," asserting her right to choose her own life instead of submitting to others' demands.
rationales.conformity: She does not want to face gatherings where relatives question "why I'm 'wasting my best years' in Dubai," showing resistance to social pressure and expected behavior.
rationales.tradition: Her reluctance to engage with family gatherings is tied to rejecting the familiar script about marriage and what a woman her age should do, which puts her at odds with inherited family expectations.
rationales.benevolence: Even while exhausted, she spent "most of yesterday holding her mother's hand while the doctors explained options," showing sustained care for someone in immediate distress.

rationales.self_direction: She pushes back against being judged for her "lifestyle" and says she "swore I would never let a job steal my life from me," framing her choices around living on her own terms.
rationales.hedonism: She defends "taking care of myself" and rejects the idea that sacrificing all pleasure and rest is morally superior.
rationales.security: Her father's heart attack made her promise she would never end up like that, showing a strong drive to protect her health and avoid a life-threatening pattern.
rationales.conformity: She rejects the workplace norm that worth is proven through total self-sacrifice, questioning why self-care is treated as "selfishness."
rationales.benevolence: She highlights staying late to comfort a frightened child and holding a mother's hand for hours, which are concrete acts of care for vulnerable people.

rationales.stimulation: Booking a "weekend trip to Muscat" and anticipating a resort stay points toward seeking novelty and a break from routine.
rationales.hedonism: She is "counting down the days" to an infinity-pool resort getaway, making pleasure and relaxation the clear focus.

rationales.stimulation: The trip included a fish market, souk, and travel to Muscat, all of which show active engagement with new places and experiences.
rationales.hedonism: She explicitly insists that "Rest is doing something. Enjoying the moment is doing something," making pleasure and recovery central rather than incidental.
rationales.benevolence: She wanted Sara to believe that rest counts because "If I can help her let go of that even a little, maybe she won't burn out," which is direct care for a close friend's wellbeing.

rationales.self_direction: She is clear that marrying someone after three months and quitting work to "focus on 'starting a family'" is "not the life I want," which is a direct assertion of her own path.
rationales.conformity: Even though she stays polite outwardly, the entry frames family pressure as something they expect her to absorb, and she internally rejects that script.
rationales.tradition: She pushes against the family's preferred model of marriage and gender roles, treating it as an inherited expectation that does not fit her life.

rationales.self_direction: She defines a life she personally wants rather than adopting someone else's metric, saying "I know which one I want on my deathbed."
rationales.hedonism: She explicitly values "good meals, beautiful sunsets, moments of genuine laughter with people I love" over constant work and prestige.
rationales.achievement: She contrasts her values with people who count "achievements, promotions, publications" and clearly rejects organizing her life around those markers.
rationales.benevolence: The life she prefers is measured partly by "moments of genuine laughter with people I love," showing that close relationships are central to what she values.
Her grief is rooted in the personal care she gave the child, from "adjusting her pillows" to bringing "extra apple juice" and noticing the girl asking whether she had eaten lunch.

rationales.hedonism: The entry celebrates a rooftop dinner, marina walk, and the belief that "the work will always be there. The moments won't," which clearly privileges enjoyment and present-moment pleasure.
rationales.benevolence: She encouraged Nadia not to cancel and defended taking time for herself because work had been "crushing her," showing care for a friend's emotional wellbeing.

rationales.hedonism: She is looking forward to "sleeping in and maybe getting a massage" and says "That's enough," making comfort and pleasure the explicit goal.

rationales.self_direction: She answered her aunt by saying "I'm very happy with my life as it is" and ended by affirming she is "living the life I actually want," which is a direct claim to self-authored choices.
rationales.hedonism: Her father's lesson that "life is for living" and her closing image of going straight to the beach reinforce a clear orientation toward enjoying life rather than deferring it.
rationales.conformity: She stops apologizing or getting defensive in response to family pressure, resisting the expectation that she should explain herself to satisfy others.
rationales.tradition: The conflict centers on refusing the customary script of "settling down," putting her stance at odds with the family's established expectations.

rationales.universalism: What matters to him is that "the kids were using it during lunch breaks," showing that the work's value comes from helping underserved students beyond his immediate circle.

rationales.security: He says he is "relieved one less thing is on the table financially," which directly frames the event in terms of reduced uncertainty and greater stability.
rationales.benevolence: His reaction is tied to family welfare, especially concern for Priya and household finances, rather than personal gain.

rationales.security: The entry is dominated by worries about salary, "the math I keep avoiding," and the cost of Amma's consultation, all of which reflect concern with financial safety and stability.
rationales.benevolence: His unfinished question is driven by Amma's worsening knees and how to help cover what she needs, showing concrete concern for a close family member.

rationales.security: He is troubled by whether he can afford Amma's surgery if he stays on "idealistic-startup money," which makes stability and material protection a clear concern.
rationales.benevolence: The dilemma is painful because he fears he will "failed someone," especially with respect to Amma's knee and his obligations to family.
rationales.universalism: He weighs family needs against "the 2G kids in Hubli," showing real concern for people outside his immediate circle who would be affected by his choices.

rationales.security: He is drawn to a role where "the pay would be significantly better" and keeps framing the choice around risk, timing, and whether he finally has enough breathing room.
rationales.universalism: He worries that if the handoff fails, "actual kids lose access," making the impact on a broader vulnerable group central to his decision.

rationales.benevolence: The emotional weight comes from Amma wondering whether he is "overworking myself for her sake," implying that his choices are being shaped by concern for his mother.
What moves him is that Farah built the project because "her grandmother needed help," and he responds to that close, caring motivation as "the whole thing, right there."
rationales.universalism: He reconnects with why he started the bootcamp through a project aimed at helping people in the community, showing commitment to using skills for broader social good.

rationales.power: He is managing Nadia's options on his timetable, saying they would "discuss it seriously in June," which shows a need to control the family decision.
rationales.security: He frames saying no as risking whether "the business is not enough to hold the family together," treating family stability as the central concern.
rationales.tradition: He measures the situation against "the way it held us when Baba was running it," anchoring the decision in continuity with his father's model.

rationales.power: He is preoccupied with who will "carry it forward" and frustrated that Nadia will not even "visit the warehouse," showing a desire to direct the family's future and succession.
rationales.security: The repeated question of "what happens to this company in fifteen years" shows a strong focus on long-term continuity and protection from instability.
rationales.tradition: He treats inheriting the business as a duty, saying "I did not choose this either, but I showed up" and "there was no question to ask," which aligns with honoring established family expectations.

rationales.self_direction: He resists the distributor's push to rebrand, saying he "almost hung up on him," which shows an independent refusal to let outsiders dictate the company's path.
rationales.tradition: He defends the family crest because "My father designed that label" and asks what remains if he strips away what connects the company to its origins.

rationales.security: He says the thread holds together "Everything" and fears being "the one who finally broke the thread," showing a strong need for continuity and protection from rupture.
rationales.tradition: He reads Baba's notebooks at night and frames changing the business as a "betrayal" of how his father built it, which clearly honors inherited ways.

rationales.power: He describes himself as the one who must "hold the center" and be "the anchor," showing a need to maintain authority over the family's direction.
rationales.security: He frames his role as keeping everyone from drifting and "keep[ing] things together here," which centers stability over individual freedom.
rationales.tradition: He ties his identity to staying, being the eldest, and preserving "the one plan I had for this family's future," reflecting loyalty to inherited family structure.

rationales.benevolence: Despite a hard workweek, he drove to Irbid because "Mama's water heater broke," prioritizing a close family member's need over his own rest.

rationales.tradition: He focuses on Rania using "Mama's recipe" and says it reminded him that "some things do carry forward," explicitly valuing inherited family practices.

rationales.achievement: He responds to flat growth by approving Samir's expansion proposal, taking concrete action to improve the business rather than staying passive.
rationales.security: He justifies diversification because the pipeline is "fragile," showing a protective move to reduce risk and stabilize the company.

rationales.conformity: He insists that giving was not heroism but obligation, saying "you give because that is what you do," which emphasizes following communal expectations.
rationales.tradition: He takes the children to the old mosque tied to his father and worries that these norms are "not as obvious to them as it was to us," showing commitment to passing inherited ways on.

rationales.benevolence: He listens to Nadia talk "through dinner" and feels "pride," showing emotional investment in a close family member's flourishing.

rationales.security: He cannot answer London because he keeps feeling "this weight in my chest" about the company "ten years from now with no family in it," showing concern with long-term stability.
rationales.tradition: Even with a compromise mockup, he evaluates everything through what Baba built and who has "the right to decide what stayed and what evolved," centering inherited continuity.

rationales.self_direction: He stops waiting for certainty and "made the decision with it still there," showing a deliberate personal choice rather than continued deference to inherited pressure.
rationales.tradition: Letting Nadia "apply to London" abandons the succession path he had been trying to preserve for the family business, so the choice conflicts with continuity.
rationales.benevolence: He allows the path that makes Nadia cry with relief, prioritizing his daughter's wellbeing and aspirations over his preferred family plan.

rationales.stimulation: She dreads going "back to the schedule" and misses mornings with "No agenda," which shows routine and predictability feel constraining rather than enlivening.
rationales.hedonism: What she cherishes is sensory ease and enjoyment: "coffee the way I like it, with cardamom," fruit on the balcony, and "sun and sticky fingers."
She chooses immediate comfort by eating "leftover knafeh for breakfast" and watching a cooking show for two hours instead of doing something productive.

rationales.self_direction: She carves out a private ritual after drop-off and describes it as "reclaiming something small," which signals taking back authorship over her own time.
rationales.hedonism: The entry centers pleasure and ease: she drinks cardamom coffee "slowly on the balcony" and values "Twenty minutes of nothing."

rationales.self_direction: She cannot give a direct answer and admits "that silence is going to cost me later," showing she is not acting freely in line with her own preference.
rationales.hedonism: She says Saturdays are "the one day we have no obligations" and resists losing that last pocket of enjoyable freedom.
rationales.conformity: Her expectation of a lecture about "what a good mother would do" shows she is restraining herself around family norms to avoid disapproval.

rationales.hedonism: She describes six hours of social performance and says the bath afterward "was the best part of the entire day," suggesting the family obligation crowded out enjoyment.
rationales.conformity: She spends the day "Smiling, helping in the kitchen, making sure the children behave, agreeing" in order to meet family expectations and keep things smooth.

rationales.hedonism: She dwells on the pleasure of "pistachio gelato," a warm evening, and being able to "not think about anything else," making enjoyment the point of the entry.
rationales.benevolence: What moves her most is "my kids being happy" and a family moment where nobody asks her to be anything else, showing fulfillment through close loved ones' wellbeing.

rationales.self_direction: She asserts personal choice by saying "I don't need to justify it," rejecting the idea that this small purchase requires anyone else's approval.
rationales.hedonism: The purchase is openly about enjoyment and comfort, capped by "The candle smells incredible though."

rationales.self_direction: She says every open space gets "assigned a purpose by someone else" and fears "disappearing entirely into the role," showing a loss of personal autonomy.
rationales.hedonism: She mourns the loss of time for "something unstructured, something that just feels good," so the situation clearly works against present enjoyment.
rationales.conformity: She cannot say this aloud because it would sound like she does not care about her children's upbringing, which shows active self-restraint around social expectations.

rationales.hedonism: She neglected her own comfort, saying she "ate cold rice standing at the counter" after spending the evening helping others.
rationales.achievement: She "stayed late" trying to stretch limited materials across her classroom, showing strong effort to meet professional standards despite obstacles.
rationales.benevolence: She kept helping both her students and Sofia, noting "she called me, and I picked up" even when it cost her time and rest.

rationales.security: She feels relief in the stable fact that "She's here. She's okay" and centers the safety of being "together" at home.
rationales.tradition: The familiar bedtime exchange, "buenas noches, mija," is presented as a grounding family ritual that carries meaning.
rationales.benevolence: Her attention settles on closeness with Mama and Sofia, and she frames the important thing as "right now, in this house, we're together."

rationales.self_direction: She resists others' expectations about the department head role and asserts, "I made the right choice. I know I did."
rationales.benevolence: She dismisses her cousin's advice because she has "no one depending on her," showing the decision was shaped by responsibility to close others.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects the more managerial path, saying "Not running meetings about assessment metrics" and recommits to the work that feels right to her.
rationales.benevolence: She is deeply moved by Andres finding his voice and defines her purpose as "watching a kid find his voice, being there for that moment."
She takes genuine joy in Sofia's improvement, reflecting that "Maybe I helped get her there" and smiling at the message for a full minute.
He goes beyond the repair session by giving Fatima his email for the teenager who wants to learn, offering direct support to someone specific.
rationales.universalism: He refurbishes laptops for families who need them and says handing over "a working machine to someone who actually needs it" is what makes the work meaningful.

rationales.benevolence: He responds to Ahmad's interest by sending "some links to freeCodeCamp," investing time in one person's growth.
rationales.universalism: His mind goes from the laptop project to "e-waste dumping in Ghana," showing concern for wider environmental harm beyond his immediate circle.

rationales.benevolence: He lingers on Ahmad's first webpage and values the teen's excitement enough to "stare at it for a while," showing warm investment in his progress.

rationales.conformity: He suppresses what he wants to say in the meeting because last time a colleague "rolled his eyes," so he "just nodded and wrote my tickets."
rationales.universalism: He immediately interprets the route work as "fewer emissions per delivery," showing concern for broader environmental impact.

rationales.conformity: He explicitly withholds his criticism, saying "I'd never say any of this to her," to avoid disrupting the relationship.
rationales.benevolence: He tells Elsa "congratulations and meant it" and stays focused on supporting her feelings rather than unloading his concerns.
rationales.universalism: His unease centers on the larger effects of "fast fashion brands" and garments that "bin half of it in six months," not just personal consequences.

rationales.achievement: He takes pride in delivering the feature to staging and quantifies the result as "roughly 14% on redundant kilometres per week."
rationales.universalism: Even after the deploy, he focuses on the wider benefit and calculates that the change also cuts carbon output: "That's something. Not nothing."

rationales.conformity: He stays quiet about the recycling comments because speaking would cost "Margareta's peace" and maybe "Elsa's evening," so he "eat[s] the strawberry."
rationales.benevolence: He restrains himself specifically to protect other people's comfort at the gathering, prioritizing their evening over his urge to argue.
rationales.universalism: He wants to defend the broader environmental stakes, thinking about "the Baltic" and why the complaint about recycling rules misses the real issue.

rationales.conformity: He explains his silence in terms of social pressure, worrying he'd be "the difficult one again" who "makes gatherings tense."
rationales.universalism: His distress is tied to collective harms like "The sea is warming" and "fish stocks are collapsing," showing concern beyond personal inconvenience.

rationales.benevolence: He agrees to write the reference "immediately" because "that kid deserves someone putting words on paper for him," directly backing a vulnerable person.
rationales.universalism: He is anguished by a system where a teenager's future depends on "a bureaucratic lottery," framing the case as a broader injustice.

rationales.benevolence: He spends care on Ahmad's letter through "Four drafts" and says the finished letter matters more than his deployment work.
rationales.universalism: He justifies staying in his job partly because "the route module does reduce emissions," keeping the wider environmental impact in view.

rationales.benevolence: He keeps checking his phone because Ahmad's hearing is next week, showing ongoing concern for one specific family.
rationales.universalism: The entry is driven by anguish over "people are drowning trying to reach exactly this kind of shore," and he continues showing up for the laptop group because helping "one family" still matters.

rationales.benevolence: He stays emotionally and practically engaged with Ahmad, noting the message "still here" and planning to keep helping through the laptop group.
rationales.universalism: He is preoccupied with systemic waste and displacement, from ships carrying goods "destined for landfill" to the hearing that keeps him thinking about more than his own life.

rationales.self_direction: He frames his path as "building something" outside the degree track his family keeps pushing, showing commitment to his own chosen route.

rationales.power: He is upset that others still see him as "some kid running errands on a scooter" and wants recognition as someone substantial and in charge.

rationales.benevolence: He agreed to Sami's request to take Friday off "for a family thing" even though it made his own day harder, showing care toward someone in his orbit.

rationales.achievement: He secured the restaurant account because "my prices were better than the other guy's," showing competitive competence and a concrete business win.
rationales.power: What bothers him most is not just the sale but that he wasn't treated like "the one making decisions," showing strong concern with status and authority.

rationales.hedonism: He turned down football even though "the routes were fine" because he did not want to step away, choosing vigilance over enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: He takes pride in operational success like "all three scooters running," drivers hitting targets, and feeling like he is "running something real."
rationales.power: He likes being the one tracking every route and says he wants to be "right there watching it happen," which reflects a need to stay in control.
rationales.security: "I don't trust good days to last if I'm not paying attention" shows a strong pull toward preserving stability and guarding against disruption.
rationales.benevolence: He rewarded Sami and Yousef with "a small bonus this week," which is a concrete supportive act toward close workers.

rationales.power: He says that in his father's shop he is "just his son moving boxes" and that he "shrink[s] in that space," describing a situation where his sense of influence and standing is undermined.
rationales.tradition: He agrees to help because "Baba asked if I could help in the shop tomorrow because his back is bad again," reflecting family-duty obligations.
rationales.benevolence: Even though he was annoyed, he still said yes to helping his father when his "back is bad again," which is a clear supportive response to a close family member.

rationales.power: The loss hurts because it feels like "what I built wasn't worth staying for," showing that his reaction is tied to whether his enterprise commands loyalty and respect.
rationales.security: He immediately translates the resignation into "losing a rider means losing a route means losing income," framing it as a threat to stability.

rationales.stimulation: He rejects the safe option because "reliable is boring" and prefers the more intense, riskier candidate who is "hungrier."
rationales.achievement: He wants workers who "want to prove something" and keeps talking about people who understand "what we're building," which centers ambition and performance.
rationales.power: He is actively screening, selecting, and mentally planning routes for hires, showing a strong orientation toward directing others and shaping the operation.
rationales.security: He knowingly dismisses the safer choice because "reliable is boring," which cuts against prioritizing predictability and stability.
The no-show forces him to cover "thirteen deliveries in the rain" himself, highlighting disruption, instability, and the cost of unreliable arrangements.

rationales.self_direction: He says he would "rather eat glass" than live like his cousin who asks his father permission before going anywhere, which is an explicit defense of autonomy.
rationales.power: He recognizes that he has been putting himself "at the center of everything" and ties his identity to being "in charge," but the entry is a critical reckoning with that need rather than an endorsement of it.
rationales.tradition: He rejects the conventional path his mother suggests to "find a girl and settle down like my cousin Ahmad," treating that model as intolerable.

rationales.achievement: He reports concrete growth like working with "eleven" restaurants and reflects on a successful first week from the new hire, showing continued business accomplishment.

rationales.self_direction: She relishes that the owner only asked for "something colorful" because it gives her "so much room to play," emphasizing creative freedom.
rationales.hedonism: She intentionally let herself enjoy the morning, ordering "three cups of chai" and insisting that "I just also let myself have a nice morning."
rationales.achievement: She is actively progressing the commission, noting that "the sketches are coming together" and that she also "finished the commission brief."

rationales.self_direction: She says "I KNOW this is what I'm meant to do" despite repeated pressure, showing firm commitment to her own chosen path.
rationales.conformity: She keeps having to "defend my choices" because others refuse to treat them as valid, which reflects active resistance to social expectations.
rationales.tradition: Her mother's script of "no salary, no benefits, no husband" represents a conventional life script that she is refusing rather than embracing.

rationales.hedonism: After a good day she buys herself "fancy dark chocolate on the way home because why not," explicitly rewarding herself with pleasure.
rationales.benevolence: She is teaching a group of kids, celebrates Advait's collage, and is clearly energized by helping children express themselves creatively.

rationales.self_direction: The payoff is that the mural "actually looks like what you imagined," which highlights realizing her own creative vision.
rationales.achievement: She "finished the Leopold mural," the owner loved it, and he is already discussing another location, marking a clear success and recognition of competence.

rationales.self_direction: She says she has "already considered every possible path and deliberately chosen this one," directly affirming independent choice.

rationales.conformity: "I made a CHOICE. Why is that so hard to respect?" shows open resistance to being pushed back into socially approved expectations.
rationales.tradition: She rejects advice coming through an older family-network figure and treats those inherited fears as something merely "parroted," rather than something to honor.

rationales.hedonism: "I'm running on fumes" shows that the workload has come at the expense of comfort and present enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: "Three workshops back to back this weekend" and "Good money" indicate heavy productive effort and successful output.

rationales.self_direction: Her ideal life centers on "the freedom to say no to projects that don't excite me," which is a direct statement of wanting to author her own life.

rationales.security: She defines success as having "enough commissions to stay comfortable" and allocates money toward "rent and supplies," showing attention to a stable base.
rationales.conformity: She explicitly rejects society's "very narrow definition" of success and argues that opting out of "the rat race" takes courage.
rationales.tradition: Her list of rejected success markers includes "marriage" and other standard life milestones, showing distance from conventional scripts.
rationales.benevolence: She frames the Goa trip as something shared with close friends, saying "We need this," which centers the wellbeing of her immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: "This is what I work for" and "this is exactly where I want to be" show that she is organizing life around her own chosen priorities rather than external standards.
rationales.stimulation: The Goa trip is full of novelty and vivid experience, from a "tiny beach shack" to sunset shots and sketching boats and palm shadows.
rationales.hedonism: "Zero regrets," repeated beach dinners, and "doing things that feel good" make present enjoyment the central point of the entry.
rationales.conformity: She says "The work will always be there when I get back" and treats pleasure and relationships as "the whole point," pushing against work-first norms.
rationales.benevolence: She treasures being on the beach "with people I love" and emphasizes that time with "the people who matter" is what counts most.

rationales.security: She rejects the cheaper flatshare because the lease is 'month-to-month,' the building lacks 'proper insurance,' and she wants 'a proper lease' with clear exit terms. The choice is driven by predictability and protection against future instability.

rationales.achievement: She says the group show is something she has been 'working toward since January' and feels deeply validated that 'someone picked my work.' The entry centers on sustained effort culminating in external recognition.

rationales.security: She restores the 'auto-transfer' to savings, keeps a 'general reserve,' and explicitly wants to avoid being 'stuck' if work dries up. The whole episode is about protecting herself from financial instability she remembers from her parents' hardship.

rationales.achievement: She spends the afternoon 'finishing the second piece for the Moreau show' and restarts the panel after the ink work 'came out wrong twice.' Persisting through fatigue to complete exhibition work shows a strong focus on standards and output.

rationales.security: She values the frame shop because it is 'predictable,' with 'a rhythm' and 'a floor under me' while freelance work stays uncertain. Keeping the steady shifts is framed as a source of stability and protection from chaos.

She defends having 'rent covered through March' and argues that keeping 'something steady' is not the same as not trying. The core concern is having something reliable underneath her if freelancing fails.

rationales.stimulation: She repeatedly chooses against uncertainty, saying that if she dropped 'all the safe choices' the 'maths says take the risk' but 'my body won't let me.' The entry shows active resistance to novelty and risk rather than seeking it.
rationales.achievement: She tracks freelance payments, updates her spreadsheet, and notes 'At this rate I'll hit my year-end target by November.' The entry includes clear goal-monitoring and progress toward a concrete benchmark.
rationales.security: She keeps the lease, phone provider, frame shop, and savings transfer because they offer 'certainty,' and even imagines risk with 'my chest gets tight.' Her choices consistently favor reliability over lower-cost but less predictable options.
As soon as Girard mentions he 'might cut hours,' she starts planning for outcomes from 'two, or one, or none.' The reaction is immediate preparation for possible instability and loss of income.

rationales.hedonism: He 'ate it standing at my desk between Slack messages,' turning even a meal into a rushed interruption. The entry shows present enjoyment being sidelined rather than prioritized.
rationales.achievement: The day is defined by 'back-to-back calls with the ops team' and constant work at the desk. The focus is on pushing through a demanding workload rather than easing off.

rationales.self_direction: He says, 'I didn't want to execute someone else's vision forever' and explicitly frames founding the company as wanting to make the call himself. That is a direct endorsement of autonomy over following others.
rationales.achievement: He is energized that the enterprise pivot he pushed 'for two months' was validated and that a colleague said the framing 'made sense.' The entry centers on performing well and having that competence recognized.
rationales.power: He savors that 'the team actually went quiet when I started talking' and wanted 'the room to look at me and know that I'm the one making the call.' The satisfaction comes from influence, authority, and being the decisive figure in the room.

When Vikram cut him off, 'The investor looked at him, not me, for the rest of the call,' and he 'didn't say anything.' The moment is experienced as a loss of authority and control in a status-sensitive setting.

He is frustrated that 'I'm the CEO of this company and I can't move my own roadmap forward' because an outside approver has the real leverage. The entry is dominated by feeling blocked, dependent, and unable to exert control.

What stays with him is 'the moment I saw the room shift' and watching people 'choose someone else's direction over mine.' The sting comes from losing influence and deference inside the company he built.

He is deeply affected by being dismissed as 'still early then' and compares his stature to someone younger performing for status in the room. The entry shows acute concern with rank, standing, and how others size him up.

rationales.achievement: He says what 'recharges' him is 'closing a deal, seeing our revenue numbers tick up, watching someone I hired three years ago become a team lead.' The day is organized around growth, performance, and building something successful at scale.
rationales.power: He overrides routing decisions for 'optics,' leverages 'Names matter' with investors, seats himself beside a deputy minister, and monitors competitors. The entry is saturated with status management, influence, and control over strategic relationships.

rationales.hedonism: He rejects the idea that work should be energizing, writing 'Not everything has to recharge you - that is a luxury mindset.' The statement explicitly dismisses enjoyment and replenishment as priorities.

rationales.achievement: He chooses the outsider because 'good is not enough for where we are going' and frames the decision around whether they become 'a regional leader or another footnote.' The reasoning is explicitly performance-driven and future-success oriented.
rationales.power: He favors a candidate who has 'the network, the experience at scale' and 'commands' a room, while insisting he 'cannot let' sentiment cloud the call. The decision prioritizes influence, stature, and organizational control.
rationales.benevolence: He knowingly overrides the case for Hana, even after hearing that 'loyalty should count for something,' because he will not let that concern shape the main decision. Supporting a long-time insider is treated as secondary to strategic advantage.

rationales.conformity: He approved Jenna leaving early and accepted being "short-staffed at close" during a surprise visit, prioritizing a person's need over expected staffing norms.
rationales.benevolence: He said yes "without even thinking about it" and took on "her side work plus mine" so Jenna could help her stranded roommate.

rationales.conformity: Instead of preserving the schedule, he "sent him home" and chose to absorb the disruption himself, going against the expectation to keep coverage intact.
rationales.benevolence: After learning Tommy's grandpa died, he "told him I'd cover" and framed it as doing "the thing you'd want someone to do for you."
He "sent three emails and sat in a forty-five minute call with regional" to try to get Priya promoted and keeps replaying the conversation because he feels he let her down.

rationales.conformity: He defended approving "too many shift swaps" and pushed back on the district manager instead of simply accepting the corporate line.
rationales.benevolence: His response was that "my team has lives outside of work," showing he is protecting employees' personal needs.
He treats Jenna remembering his coffee order as meaningful because "sometimes it's just about people noticing each other," emphasizing mutual care over tasks.

rationales.hedonism: Helping Maria is costing him "my sister's birthday dinner," "meals I skip," and "sleep I don't get," showing repeated sacrifice of present comfort and enjoyment.
rationales.security: He stayed because "I wasn't going to make her walk home in the dark. It's not safe," directly prioritizing protection from harm.
rationales.benevolence: He stayed "two hours late" with Maria even though it was "not in my job description," because she was nineteen, stranded, and dealing with her mom's chemo bills.
He says "that's why I do it" after learning Priya stayed because "of how we treat people here," and centers making others feel "seen" and like they matter.
After work, they spent the evening "helping Amma sort the kitchen," showing practical care for a close family member.
They "sat with her for almost an hour" and could not move on from Fatima until she managed the first letters of her name.
rationales.universalism: They willingly take "that awful bus that takes ninety minutes one way" to a literacy camp because helping a child learn to write her name feels like the point of the work.
They are encouraged that a lesson on "why their water is dirty" got a child talking to his family, and describe that broader civic awareness as "a small thread pulling in the right direction."

rationales.benevolence: After finishing school data work, they "stayed back to help the peon uncle fix a broken desk," offering hands-on help beyond their own duties.

rationales.conformity: They "kept quiet because Sharma sir is senior" and did not challenge him, deferring to hierarchy to avoid conflict.
rationales.universalism: When a senior teacher dismissed poor parents as not valuing education, they "just sat there" despite knowing the comment was unfair to marginalized families.
They chose the convenient chain store over the local women's self-help group and say it felt like failing to "put money where my mouth is" on fairness.

rationales.self_direction: They resisted family pressure to pursue a "better school" path and told Baba, "I like my school" and "I'm okay right now," affirming an independent choice.
rationales.universalism: They used unexpected free time to plan a unit on "water and sanitation" because students carrying those conversations home showed the topic could matter beyond the classroom.

rationales.hedonism: She canceled the Bandra dinner she had been anticipating and notes she was "exhausted and hungry," putting immediate enjoyment aside.
rationales.security: She explicitly says, "I don't like the uncertainty. The current schedule works for me," showing a strong preference for predictability and stability.
rationales.tradition: Her choice is reinforced by "My mother always said that when someone is drowning in grief, you throw them a rope," invoking inherited family guidance.
rationales.benevolence: She stayed "an extra forty-five minutes helping them with paperwork" because the grieving family was alone and leaving "felt wrong."

rationales.self_direction: She describes her life being rearranged without input, learning via WhatsApp that she was moved after "five years of building my life around that schedule."
rationales.stimulation: The change is framed as deeply unwelcome disruption rather than energizing novelty; she wanted her established day-shift routine, not a new rotation.
rationales.hedonism: The roster change threatens the Goa trip where she planned to "sit on the beach and eat fish curry and do absolutely nothing productive for three days."
rationales.security: Being shifted unexpectedly and told only through the group chat undercuts the stable arrangement she had built her life around.

rationales.self_direction: She pushes back directly, explaining that "my life outside these walls is mine to plan" and asserting her right to shape it herself.
rationales.stimulation: She wants "some predictability" and resents having everything changed by someone else's spreadsheet, showing clear aversion to disruption and uncertainty.
rationales.hedonism: Keeping Goa required a costly trade that already gave up "a holiday," showing leisure and enjoyment being constrained rather than pursued.
rationales.security: She emphasizes promised "schedule stability," worries about health and family routines, and repeatedly asks for predictability and something she can plan around.

rationales.achievement: He immediately moves to the numbers and how to keep the office profitable, treating the call as a high-stakes business performance problem rather than a personal slight alone.
rationales.power: He is fixated on being publicly cornered and says, "You don't ambush the person who signs your contracts," showing a strong need to preserve authority and control.
rationales.conformity: He is not trying to smooth things over; he wants to "walk this back" and imagines making Minh come to "my building, at my table," which pushes against cooperative norm-maintenance.
rationales.benevolence: Even after admitting the proposal was reasonable, he centers what he "can't let stand" and how to reassert leverage, not the partner's needs or the relationship.

rationales.self_direction: He takes matters into his own hands by booking the trip himself and setting the terms of the encounter instead of waiting for the other side's process.
rationales.power: "Didn't mention the revenue share. Let him wonder" shows he is deliberately using uncertainty to reestablish control over the situation and the other person.
rationales.conformity: Rather than signaling openness or collaboration, he withholds the real agenda and turns the visit into a pressure move, which cuts against straightforward social harmony.
rationales.benevolence: The choice to "let him wonder" is aimed at unsettling Minh, not supporting or understanding him.

rationales.achievement: He frames the trip as a win because "Minh pulled back to a five-percent adjustment instead of twelve" and "the office stays profitable," emphasizing successful business results.
rationales.power: He is satisfied that "the meeting went how I wanted" and that he "steered the conversation," showing a clear focus on directing others and controlling the outcome.
rationales.benevolence: He recognizes Minh "went along with it because he had to" and that he may be the last person called in a real problem, but he accepts that cost to get what he needed.

rationales.power: It "felt good to be the one they came to," especially because of his reputation, which shows satisfaction in status and influence.
rationales.benevolence: He is being asked to mentor younger founders on their pitch, which is a direct offer of help and support to people in his network.

rationales.power: He defends the elite school because of "the name and the network" and the doors it opens, showing strong concern with status-linked advantage and influence.
rationales.benevolence: He wires the tuition "immediately" and says the whole point is "so she never has to struggle the way I did," which is clear care for his daughter's welfare.
He reaches out with a small affectionate gesture, sending "a photo of the dog sleeping on her old blanket," and lingers over her heart emoji because the connection matters to him.

rationales.hedonism: After closing the deal he is too depleted to enjoy anything: "Not celebration, just the weight coming off" and "I can't even turn on the TV."
rationales.achievement: The entry centers on enduring "twelve-hour days" and closing the Series C extension while "held the line on the terms I wanted," showing sustained pursuit of high performance.
rationales.power: He emphasizes holding firm against investors and later says real celebration would be someone recognizing "what it took," tying the effort to standing and authority.

rationales.achievement: He is preoccupied with "exceeded expectations on every metric" and being noticed for the "regional director track," which shows strong investment in external success.
rationales.power: He withholds credit because he does not want to "make myself look like I need hand-holding from my own team," showing concern with rank and authority.
rationales.benevolence: He knowingly omits Ahmed's contribution even while admitting it was "Ahmed's suggestion originally," prioritizing his own advancement over fair treatment of a subordinate.

rationales.hedonism: The whole entry is about pleasure and recovery: "Pool day Saturday, Nobu Saturday night, recovery brunch Sunday" and "Needed that reset."

rationales.power: He is outraged at having to justify himself and says, "I'm not here to be liked. I'm here to deliver," which frames leadership in terms of authority and command.
rationales.conformity: He rejects open challenge and tells Rami concerns should come "privately," resisting a norm of transparent, collaborative discussion in the group.
rationales.tradition: He explicitly roots his view in inherited male authority: "My father always says" and "the men I respected ran their households and their businesses the same way."

rationales.hedonism: He chooses a flashy night out because he wanted "good food" and values the immediate payoff enough to consider putting the next party "on the card and deal with it later."
rationales.power: He picked the expensive restaurant because he wanted people "to see us there," and the gesture of paying is tied to looking like a leader with status.
rationales.security: He admits his account will be "tight until the 25th" and may still charge more leisure spending, which actively deprioritizes financial stability.

rationales.power: He defends his spending as "building relationships that will pay off later" and insists status networking is necessary, showing strong orientation toward influence and standing.
rationales.security: When pushed to save for "a proper apartment," he dismisses it and keeps prioritizing image-building over stability.

rationales.hedonism: He misses the night he wanted and ends up "eating salmon rolls under fluorescent lights" instead of going out, showing pleasure sacrificed to work pressure.
rationales.achievement: He stays until "10pm" salvaging the proposal and measures the night in terms of keeping the deal alive, which reflects strong commitment to getting results.
rationales.power: "Either I control everything and get called difficult, or I trust people and they drop the ball" shows a strong pull toward maintaining personal control over outcomes.

rationales.hedonism: He deliberately treats himself to "a table by the pool at Sofitel, unlimited sparkling and eggs royale" and dwells on how good the food and sun felt.
rationales.power: He admits he wants "proof" through "the photos, the posts, the reservations at places with waitlists," showing that status display and being seen are central to the experience.

rationales.achievement: "Got the regional recognition for Q2 results" and a plaque at all-hands directly reflect striving for and receiving recognized success.
rationales.power: The photo "with the CEO" and the fact he is "already thinking about what's next" show the recognition matters as part of climbing higher.
He admits, "She makes me look good. We look good together. That matters in this city," treating the relationship partly as a status asset.
rationales.benevolence: He keeps cancelling and avoids the honest conversation he knows she wants, prioritizing his own convenience over her clarity and feelings.

rationales.power: He still evaluates life through "status," "visibility," salary, and a "corner office," and the dive-shop owner's lack of those markers is exactly what unsettles him.

rationales.achievement: He is relieved because the contract closes after "months of quoting and following up," marking a concrete business win.
rationales.security: He says the contract's value is that "it's recurring revenue" and that it "covers his salary through next winter," clearly prioritizing predictability and stability.
rationales.benevolence: The weight is about "keep[ing] the lights on for the guys who depend on me," showing concern for close others who rely on him.

rationales.self_direction: He resists the recruiter because "walking away from it for a salary feels like admitting something I'm not ready to admit," showing attachment to the independent path he built himself.
rationales.security: He repeatedly checks the bank balance and notes the numbers look fine when he checks them "four times a day," revealing a strong focus on financial steadiness.
