rationales.self_direction: She rejects the conventional comparison point, saying "my path looks nothing like that and I'm fine with it."
rationales.security: By contrasting herself with a stable graduate program and affirming a very different path, she actively deprioritizes predictability and conventional stability.

rationales.self_direction: She resists pressure to commercialize the platform, admitting she may be "stubborn" but still refusing options that would change its purpose.
rationales.security: She acknowledges "I can't keep bleeding money either" while continuing an unsustainable setup, showing conflict with stability.
rationales.benevolence: Her attention keeps returning to Minjun and how to protect him, even saying she would hide the uncertainty because "he's fourteen."
rationales.universalism: She insists the service stay free so "nobody has to qualify or prove they deserve help," reflecting concern for fair access beyond her immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: She says the fight would start when she tells her mother "I don't regret leaving school," reaffirming her own chosen path.
rationales.security: The entry is saturated with instability: "haven't slept properly," a freelance deadline, empty tutor spots, and her mother's worries about her having "nothing stable."

rationales.achievement: She solved the staffing problem and "stayed up until 2am onboarding" the replacement tutor, showing sustained competence and effort.
rationales.benevolence: She found a replacement for Minjun and recognizes Soyeon's support, ending with "I need to be better at letting people help me," which opens her more to caring relationships.

rationales.self_direction: She deletes "I'm in!" three times and says if she committed she would have to "actually make a choice," showing avoidance of acting on her own wants.
rationales.hedonism: She wants the spa and admits "my body hurts" and she misses laughing with friends, but decides she will "probably say no" and deny herself the pleasure.
rationales.conformity: She centers what "Mummyji will do" and how asking for time for herself would make her "the difficult one," so she leans into the expected self-sacrificing role.
rationales.benevolence: Her whole calculus is organized around childcare and family logistics, including who will give Arjun milk and whether Aanya will nap for anyone else.

rationales.hedonism: She longs to "lie down somewhere quiet" and envies the spa day, but her own comfort is completely sidelined.
rationales.conformity: She keeps nodding "yes, good idea" to remedies she does not believe in because she lacks the energy to push back against household expectations.
rationales.benevolence: She has been caring for two sick children since 3am, cleaning vomit and managing the day largely on her own.

rationales.self_direction: She says "somewhere along the way I stopped asking" and learned to make herself not need things, which suppresses her own agency.
rationales.hedonism: Her only admitted pleasure is "twenty minutes of silence with a warm cup," and even that is framed as maybe all she is allowed to have.
rationales.conformity: She describes shrinking her needs into "smaller things that nobody would notice," adapting herself to avoid disrupting others' expectations.

rationales.self_direction: She chooses what she genuinely wants over the social default, saying "I wanted this more" and that the balcony "doesn't ask anything from me."
rationales.hedonism: The entry lingers on sensory enjoyment: "a glass of something cold," golden light, bread in the air, and a quiet evening she deliberately preferred.

rationales.self_direction: She says she "didn't push back at all" and left feeling like she'd "agreed to be someone I'm not," which shows acting against her own judgment.
rationales.security: She explicitly withholds her view because her mentor is "the one signing off on my certification," prioritizing a safe outcome.
rationales.conformity: She smiles, nods, and plans to comply because she does not want to be "difficult" and notes that everyone else "just does what they're told."

rationales.self_direction: She says using the stricter approach "felt horrible" yet still did it, acting against her own teaching instincts.
rationales.conformity: She enforced the rule even though "my mentor wasn't even watching," showing internalized compliance with expected behavior.
rationales.benevolence: She recognizes that making the child wait in silence meant "I'd taken something away from him," prioritizing compliance over the child's comfort.

rationales.security: She carefully edits what she tells her mother to avoid opening "that door" into worry and upheaval, aiming to keep the relationship predictable and calm.
rationales.conformity: She and her mother "performed their part" and she treats this calibrated, expectation-managing interaction as normal family behavior.

rationales.hedonism: She savors "pistachio macarons," the balcony, and the feeling that "my head is okay," making room for simple pleasure after a hard day.
rationales.achievement: She has been "prepping materials every evening" for assessments and takes real pride in a concrete learning success during the reading task.
rationales.conformity: In the tense staff meeting she stayed silent because "that's my place right now" and it was "smarter to just listen," deferring to role expectations.
rationales.benevolence: The emotional center of the entry is Nour reading aloud and Lucie holding onto that child's breakthrough with obvious care.

rationales.stimulation: She avoids the party and the social unpredictability around it, retreating from a more novel and lively option.
rationales.hedonism: She genuinely enjoys staying in with "leftover macarons" and a house-renovation show, preferring immediate comfort over the party.
rationales.conformity: She keeps replaying the earlier comment because it felt like she had "broken some rule," and now she is modifying her behavior to avoid social disapproval.

rationales.self_direction: She knows she could satisfy the rubric "if I tried harder" but refuses because "I just don't want to become that teacher," prioritizing her own educational judgment.
rationales.achievement: She is dissatisfied with "meets expectations" yet explicitly chooses not to optimize for the assessed standard or the higher rating.
rationales.benevolence: She values being the teacher who made "a scared kid feel safe enough to read out loud" and wants the classroom to remain a place children actually want to be.

rationales.hedonism: She could not even enjoy Priya's moving paper because "the stack keeps growing," and she stayed up "still marking" instead of allowing herself rest or pleasure.
rationales.achievement: "Marking forty-two exam papers" late into the evening shows sustained effort to meet demanding professional standards despite fatigue.
rationales.benevolence: In her own words, "Mummy-ji got empty promises" and she "did not even sit with Rajesh while he ate," showing close family members' needs were neglected.

rationales.conformity: She calmly "explained the rubric twice" during a contentious parent meeting, sticking to institutional expectations rather than escalating or breaking role boundaries.

rationales.benevolence: What mattered most was that Bahu "noticed" her preference and "paid attention," centering care and attunement within a close relationship.
The entry is organized around intimate family connection: she tears up hearing Aarav say "Nani," then sits quietly with Mummy-ji while Rajesh respectfully gives them space.

rationales.tradition: Her response, "It is her parents, what else would I tell her," treats caring for aging parents as an obvious obligation that should be honored.
rationales.benevolence: She "spent the morning helping Sunita draft her transfer application" so Sunita could be closer to her parents, prioritizing another person's family need over departmental convenience.

rationales.conformity: She "kept quiet" in the meeting because she did not want to "add to the noise" after the decision was already made, showing restraint to maintain order.

rationales.benevolence: She anticipated Kamla-ji's knee pain by setting up "an extra cushion" and later told Rajesh to rest because "his back has been bothering him," showing practical care for close community and family.

rationales.conformity: She holds back from signing because the principal "came to Ritu's wedding" and she fears it will "land as a personal thing," prioritizing smooth relationships and social expectations.
rationales.benevolence: Although she admits "the younger teachers are burning out," she "did not sign immediately," withholding support from close colleagues who are being harmed.

rationales.conformity: She says "what feels right is staying out of it" and resists taking a stand, favoring social comfort and non-disruption over challenge.
rationales.benevolence: She still has "not signed Neha's letter" even though the workload harms colleagues, so she is not backing people in her immediate work circle when support is needed.

rationales.self_direction: She says the curator wants "everything I've been moving away from" yet decides, "So I'll make something new," subordinating her own artistic direction to external demands.
rationales.achievement: She frames the compromise as "a good showing here means better representation, better sales, a step closer to the kind of career I've been working toward for eight years," clearly pursuing success markers.
rationales.conformity: She tells the curator she has work that will "fit perfectly" and resolves to produce what was requested because "that's what professionals do," aligning herself with gatekeeper expectations.

rationales.self_direction: The new sketches are "nothing that feels like mine," showing she is working against her own artistic authorship.
rationales.achievement: "Started three new sketches" and notes the deadline is manageable "if I stay focused," emphasizing disciplined progress toward a professional goal.
rationales.conformity: She is deliberately making work that is "everything that fits," shaping the pieces to match outside expectations rather than her own vision.

rationales.self_direction: She remembers believing she would "never compromise" but now admits her "actual work stays hidden in a corner," showing active betrayal of her own artistic path.
rationales.achievement: She keeps prioritizing what advances her career, stressing the feeder show's effect on "networking dinner and studio visit for the next two years" and that "meeting expectations has" paid the rent.
rationales.security: "Feeling right hasn't paid my rent in eight years" makes clear that financial stability is a live motive in her choice to keep complying.
rationales.conformity: She explicitly justifies compromise because "the art world runs on relationships, and relationships run on making people comfortable," showing adaptation to social rules and gatekeepers.

rationales.hedonism: "Worked twelve hours" on pieces she only calls "competent" suggests grinding through unrewarding work rather than seeking enjoyment or relief.
rationales.achievement: Spending twelve hours finishing the Rotterdam work shows strong effort toward performance and output even while feeling dissatisfied.

rationales.self_direction: Instead of revealing the work that feels alive, she suppresses it and stays with the commercial path, saying nothing about the night paintings during the review.
rationales.achievement: The meeting centers on "sales figures, upcoming opportunities" and a move that builds "long-term careers," and she leans into that by asking about "the fair calendar for next spring."
rationales.conformity: She thanks Pieter for his guidance and keeps the conversation on strategy, fitting herself to the gallery's professional script rather than voicing dissent.

rationales.achievement: She defends Rotterdam because some artists "need our art to actually function as a career," showing a strong commitment to making art succeed professionally.
rationales.conformity: She sees herself as "the person who plays by the rules" and keeps presenting herself as "excited about the opportunity," even while sensing others' judgment.

rationales.self_direction: She says the night sessions are the only time she is not "performing something," yet she remains on the path that feels externally scripted rather than self-authored.
rationales.security: "If I follow that feeling I lose everything I've built" shows her thinking is anchored in preserving stability and avoiding loss.

rationales.achievement: She completes the project efficiently: "Rotterdam pieces are done," she sent the photos, submitted documentation, and is already "moving on."
rationales.conformity: The curator says the pieces were "exactly what we discussed," confirming that she successfully matched the requested brief.

rationales.self_direction: She is deeply moved by canvases that "broke every rule about composition" and by artists "showing work they believe in," signaling attraction to more self-authored expression.
rationales.stimulation: The room's "energy," people "arguing about" the art, and the "massive chaotic canvases" all depict her feeling vividly engaged by novelty and intensity.
rationales.achievement: After reflecting on the polished Rotterdam path as "success," she undercuts it with "I keep telling myself that's what I wanted," suggesting alienation from the prestige-oriented goal she has been chasing.

rationales.self_direction: She calls the hidden canvases "my real work" yet answers "One step at a time. Rotterdam first," continuing to defer her own authentic direction to the approved path.
rationales.achievement: Despite the inner conflict, her immediate choice is still to prioritize Rotterdam, keeping the prestigious career milestone first.
rationales.conformity: Every phrase of the statement feels false because it sounds like "someone else wrote it," but she remains focused on producing the expected professional persona for the show.

rationales.stimulation: He is stuck estimating 'the same maintenance tickets' for three years and says 'that is maybe the problem,' showing clear frustration with repetition and stagnation.

rationales.self_direction: He wanted to say 'let us do it, let me learn something new' but suppressed that impulse and argued the safer line instead.
rationales.stimulation: He is drawn to 'learn something new instead of patching the same codebase forever,' but his actual choice blocks that novelty.
rationales.security: He explicitly argued they should consider 'the timeline and the risk to the December release' because 'the business needs stability.'
rationales.conformity: He takes the socially responsible role, saying 'someone had to be the adult' rather than voicing his own preference.

rationales.self_direction: Despite all the hesitation and stakeholder calculus, he makes the call himself: 'I will go to the conference. I already told Elena yes.'
rationales.stimulation: He chooses an 'unconference' with improvised sessions and says five years ago he would have booked it immediately, showing a real pull toward novelty and challenge.

rationales.self_direction: He says he keeps 'swallowing things down' and 'make[s] myself small' instead of pushing back, which shows him suppressing his own judgment.
rationales.hedonism: A 3 AM page, four hours of incident work, and being unable to sleep because his mind keeps 'rehearsing the incident' show clear sacrifice of comfort and ease.
rationales.achievement: He 'fixed it and documented everything' within hours under pressure, demonstrating strong competence and performance.
rationales.security: His response is to 'add better monitoring' and track renewal dates, focusing on preventing future risk and restoring reliability.
rationales.conformity: He gives the 'safe answer' and 'team player answer,' choosing restraint over confrontation to keep things smooth.
rationales.tradition: He frames his coping style through his father, saying 'It is what my father did at his factory job for forty years,' and now treats that inherited model with respect.

rationales.stimulation: He describes the routine as 'automatic' and says he is just watching himself repeat 'questions I have answered a hundred times,' signaling deadening repetition rather than novelty.
He flies to San Francisco for an unconference where 'people pitch sessions on index cards' and explores unfamiliar conversations, showing active engagement with new experiences.
When asked what he brought back, he reduces it to 'Some business card contacts. A reading list. The usual conference stuff,' showing retreat from turning inspiration into something new.
rationales.conformity: He avoids sharing the sessions that mattered most because 'I cannot really present [them] in a corporate setting,' adapting himself to what the workplace will accept.

rationales.self_direction: He says what he wants is 'Building something' but dismisses it as 'not realistic at my level,' yielding to role constraints instead of pursuing it.
rationales.stimulation: He contrasts real work with managing 'people's feelings about work' and status reports, showing alienation from novelty and hands-on creation.
rationales.hedonism: He is working 'ten-hour days' through a chaotic sprint, clearly giving up rest and present enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: He is pushing hard 'trying to keep us on track for the holiday release,' reflecting strong performance focus under pressure.
rationales.conformity: He is the one who has to explain decisions to Jennifer 'without making leadership look bad,' prioritizing organizational harmony and acceptable messaging.

rationales.self_direction: He acts on his own restlessness by proposing a move 'not as a lead, not as an architect. Just as a developer,' choosing the path he actually wants.
rationales.stimulation: He wants 'to learn something new' on the 'Go rewrite project' and says even floundering in a new language feels 'more alive than certainty.'
rationales.power: He explicitly asks for a move that looks 'like a demotion' and rejects the higher-status identity others expect him to keep.
rationales.security: He knowingly invites uncertainty, waiting to see whether he will 'flounder in a new language at 57' rather than staying in the safe maintenance role.

rationales.self_direction: He follows through on the transfer and starts preparing for it on his own terms, 'feeling like a student again.'
rationales.stimulation: Beginning on Jennifer's team and studying 'Go tutorials at night' shows active pursuit of novelty and challenge.

rationales.achievement: He is intensely tracking relative standing, noting Vikram's scope and planning how his 'name [will] come up naturally' for the lead role.
rationales.power: He says 'You have to make your time come' and seeks 'visibility' and leadership positioning beyond normal sprint delivery.
rationales.conformity: He says 'you do what's expected' when talking about family obligations, framing duty to others as the proper baseline.
rationales.tradition: Sending extra to the 'temple renovation fund' and describing those duties as simply 'how it should be' shows active commitment to inherited family-cultural practices.
rationales.benevolence: He sends extra money and says 'You take care of your family,' emphasizing material support for close others.

rationales.conformity: He admits 'We both know I'm stalling,' avoiding a socially expected next step in the relationship rather than meeting it directly.
rationales.tradition: Delaying taking Priya to meet his parents pushes against a customary family progression that both of them clearly recognize.

rationales.hedonism: He says 'I'd rather be indispensable, even if it costs me sleep,' explicitly sacrificing comfort and rest.
rationales.achievement: He wants to be the person nobody can replace because 'Nobody promotes the guy who's easily replaced,' tying his effort directly to advancement.
rationales.power: Preferring to be 'indispensable' reflects a desire for control and leverage within the team rather than simple helpfulness.

rationales.achievement: He immediately studies 'executive communication' and stakeholder management because 'I've never failed at something I committed to learning,' showing a strong drive to master what advancement requires.
rationales.power: He is focused on 'executive presence,' 'influence,' and how to 'package' himself for leadership, all of which are about gaining status and organizational sway.
rationales.tradition: He questions the rule he inherited from his father that 'doing excellent work should speak for itself' and worries that model hid 'a ceiling,' signaling a break from that legacy.

rationales.achievement: He treats the leadership presentation as a performance win, noting he kept it to 'seven slides' and got 'VP nodded twice. Small win.'
rationales.power: He successfully adapts his message to leadership using 'business metrics instead of technical specs,' which is aimed at upward influence.

rationales.security: He insists on 'flat, savings, stable position' before marriage, clearly prioritizing a secure foundation over immediate commitment.
rationales.conformity: He defends the delay by saying 'there's an order to these things' and expects Priya to accept that sequence.
rationales.tradition: He follows Amma's warning not to repeat a relative's rushed marriage and frames his approach around family-approved timing.

rationales.achievement: He lists his accomplishments in detail, from IIT to sending money home, and is frustrated that the 'family scoreboard' ignores what he is actually achieving.
rationales.power: He says 'I want more' and contrasts his ambitions with Karthik's lower ceiling, showing strong concern with status and upward movement.
rationales.conformity: He is not meeting the family's expected boxes and openly resists their standard for who counts as 'responsible.'
rationales.tradition: He dismisses the family's emphasis on 'marriage, children, temple visits' as a scoreboard that fails to recognize what matters to him.

rationales.hedonism: He tells Priya the new role will mean 'probably yes' to more late nights and frames it as necessary sacrifice now for later payoff.
rationales.achievement: He is meticulously preparing for the interview by reviewing contributions, documenting initiatives, and practicing how to frame his scope.
rationales.power: The whole entry centers on winning the 'platform team lead role' and speaking like an owner to senior leadership.
rationales.tradition: He leans on Appa's rule that 'the early years are for investment, the later years for enjoying the returns,' using inherited advice to justify his choices.

rationales.achievement: He evaluates the interview in terms of whether he 'did my part' after answering leadership questions well, showing a clear performance orientation.
He celebrates getting the role and being 'ahead now,' treating the promotion as proof of successful advancement.
rationales.power: He focuses on leadership fit and accountability, concluding 'maybe I just think the way leaders do,' which ties the win to authority and status.

rationales.achievement: He names the milestone precisely: 'Platform Team Lead before 32' with 'higher salary, direct reports, visibility to the C-suite.'
rationales.power: His pride in 'headcount, budget ownership, technical direction' shows strong investment in authority and organizational control.
rationales.tradition: He immediately reassures his family 'I always come for Pongal' and treats family expectations around Priya and the wedding as 'load-bearing.'
rationales.benevolence: He is trying to hold together obligations to close others, including 'send money home' and showing up for family events despite the strain.

rationales.self_direction: She pushes back on the civil-service pressure by insisting "I'm fine. The studio is fine. Rent is covered," defending the life she chose over the path her family prefers.
rationales.conformity: Instead of arguing, she says she has to "go check the kiln," showing restraint and a preference to avoid open family conflict.

rationales.tradition: She hears "halmeoni's voice" and judges the lucrative collaboration as having "felt cheap," letting inherited craft standards guide the decision.

rationales.achievement: She dwells on the residency as something she worked hard for, citing "two weeks on that application" and wanting to come back with something her family could "point to."
rationales.conformity: She suppresses her real reaction and keeps sending "congratulations emojis and exclamation marks," performing the socially expected response instead of expressing envy.

rationales.achievement: She goes back to work because "the pieces for the January show won't fire themselves" and takes pride that the jar's "walls were even" and the shoulder curve matched her intention.
rationales.tradition: Her standard for good work is tied to what "halmeoni always said" about asymmetry, showing reverence for inherited craft knowledge.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects the "practical" career advice and says, "I already have a name, it's just small," choosing her own definition of success over market pressure.
rationales.tradition: She measures a meaningful career against her grandmother's example of making useful things for the same neighborhood for forty years.

rationales.security: She immediately calculates the risk in having only "two months of rent and studio fees," showing strong attention to financial cushion and winter stability.
rationales.benevolence: Even after seeing the payment was short, she "just transferred Umma's share," prioritizing support for a close family member.

rationales.tradition: She makes "lunar new year gifts" using a celadon glaze her grandmother used and still adjusts it "the way she showed me," actively carrying forward a family practice.
rationales.benevolence: The bowls are being made specifically "for the family," which is a concrete act of care toward close others.

rationales.self_direction: She values "all the space I've made for myself out here" and resists going home because it feels like losing the independent life she built.
rationales.tradition: She wants to show Grandma that she is still connected to "the things she taught me" through her beadwork, treating inherited practice as something worth preserving.
She frames showing up for a ceremony as a non-negotiable family obligation, saying "that's what you do" and that back home "you just go."
rationales.benevolence: Her distress centers on a sick uncle and the belief that family should be there for him, showing strong concern for close kin.

rationales.tradition: She attends the ceremony, makes cedar tea correctly, hangs sweetgrass above her door, and says the answer may be "finding ways to carry it with me," all of which enact continuity with inherited practice.
rationales.benevolence: She accepts the cost of cancelling work to be with Grandma and family, and the visit is full of close relational care like Grandma holding her hand and gifting sweetgrass.

rationales.self_direction: She emphasizes that "the theoretical framing is mine" and that she "chose" to leave a salary and title for work on her own terms.
rationales.achievement: She is energized by having a "solid" outline and a chapter that "could become a standalone article," showing clear investment in excellence and visible accomplishment.

rationales.self_direction: She cannot simply "stay quiet" when the reasoning is sloppy and contrasts the lab with "my own work" where she is building something intellectually hers.
rationales.conformity: She explicitly resists the norm of "performative agreement" and says staying quiet is not something she can do.

rationales.self_direction: She notices with frustration that she is second-guessing "a decision I'm fully qualified to make" because an advisor might disapprove, and rejects that gatekeeping.
rationales.achievement: She says yes to a policy brief that offers "good money" and "my name on it," showing pursuit of recognized professional output.

rationales.self_direction: Even after the comparison to her cousin lands, she reaffirms that the PhD's value is that "I'm doing the actual thinking now" and says, "at least I chose these ones."
She firmly refuses the advisor's claim, saying "I won't" and insisting the chapter is "entirely mine."
rationales.achievement: She treats the chapter as the product of "every late night rewriting" and wants ownership of the best work she has produced.
rationales.conformity: She knowingly defies the expected grad-student role by refusing to "share credit with advisor" on work she says he did not build.

rationales.self_direction: What restores her is writing the policy brief, which feels like "muscle memory" and reminds her what it is like to work without someone deciding whether the output is worthy.
She keeps returning to whether to submit the chapter independently because with his name on it "it won't be mine anymore," prioritizing authorship and autonomy over harmony.
rationales.achievement: She takes pride in producing a "clean argument, clear recommendations" in the policy brief and treats the chapter as "the best thing I've written."
rationales.conformity: Submitting independently would break the safer expectation to wait and preserve the relationship, and she is seriously considering that breach.

rationales.self_direction: She wanted to respond "something sharp" but shut it down, suppressing her own view instead of expressing it.
rationales.conformity: She stays silent because speaking up "would've made dinner weird and Dan's mom was right there," clearly prioritizing social smoothness.

rationales.self_direction: She insists "I chose this" and resents others treating her life "like a problem to solve," defending a path she deliberately selected.
rationales.achievement: She wants her work to "count" and longs for someone to say "good job" or "thank you," showing a need for competence to be recognized.
rationales.benevolence: Her daily labor is centered on caring for close others through "two kids, the house, Dan's travel schedule, appointments, groceries, meal prep."

rationales.self_direction: She "just agreed with whatever she said" even though it was not her real opinion, showing automatic surrender of her own preference.
rationales.conformity: She explains that disagreeing made her feel like she'd be "the difficult one," so she defaults to agreement to avoid social friction.

rationales.self_direction: She realizes she "committed us before I even thought about what I wanted," showing her own wishes were overridden automatically.
rationales.hedonism: What she wanted was "just a quiet day" with her family, but she gives that up and frames enjoyment as something to swallow rather than choose.
rationales.conformity: She keeps the dinner commitment because backing out "would be a thing" and she does not want to seem selfish or create interpersonal tension.
rationales.benevolence: She accepts the plan partly because "there's a new baby to meet" and because "the kids love seeing their cousins," prioritizing close family relationships.

rationales.self_direction: She says, "I never said it. I never say it," highlighting a repeated pattern of not voicing her own wants.
rationales.hedonism: She gives up what she wanted and ends up "sits alone in the kitchen feeling resentful," showing present enjoyment is being denied rather than pursued.
rationales.conformity: She explicitly avoided saying "let me check with Dan first" because it would have felt like "making waves," even when nobody might have objected.

rationales.stimulation: He rejected the cheaper option because the packaging was "unfamiliar," showing avoidance of novelty rather than openness to it.
rationales.security: He spent "twenty minutes" comparing brands and chose the recognizable medication, prioritizing safety and predictability over saving money.
He describes a "hard wall" and says he'd rather be "isolated than exposed," clearly protecting himself from financial risk.
rationales.benevolence: A close family member asked for help, but he prioritizes distance over support, framing the choice as accepting isolation rather than extending care.

rationales.stimulation: He resists the uncertain move to Jeddah, saying staying put is "not gambling what already works," which rejects novelty and challenge.
rationales.security: He treats the promotion-track opportunity mainly as a risk review, emphasizing that preserving what already works matters more than the upside.
He meal-prepped for the week and refreshed the "emergency drawer," making tomorrow manageable through preparation and protection against problems.
Checking the order "three times" and linking small mistakes to feeling "like I'm standing on a ledge" shows a strong need to prevent things from going wrong.
He defends "hold[ing] on the right amount" because otherwise "things fall apart," revealing a strong preference for control and stability.
rationales.conformity: His sister thought he was "overstepping," and he still justifies contacting her ex, showing disregard for others' boundaries and social restraint.

rationales.achievement: He points to concrete competence at work, saying he runs "a team of fourteen people" and that things do not fall through because he follows up.
rationales.security: He frames his carefulness as the reason problems are prevented, emphasizing checking and follow-through to keep operations stable.
A mere rumor sends him into contingency planning, and even with "six months of expenses saved" he still feels the "floor could always open," showing intense concern with safety and stability.

rationales.self_direction: She values students debating instead of waiting for the "right" answer, which reflects support for independent thinking and choice.
rationales.achievement: Calling it a "Good class" and taking pride in students engaging differently shows a strong sense of effective performance in her role.

rationales.conformity: When the principal told her to "tone down the activism," she "nodded" and treated it as "just politics," restraining open resistance to fit the institution.
rationales.universalism: She pushes back on teaching that ocean pollution is about "foreign problems," explicitly connecting it to the local coastline and broader environmental concern.

rationales.conformity: She decides to be "strategic" and teach in ways that "don't raise red flags," adapting herself to institutional constraints rather than confronting them directly.
rationales.universalism: Her main reason for staying is that "these kids are asking questions about their own coastline," showing commitment to environmental and civic concern beyond herself.

rationales.tradition: Missing the Sunday visit feels like she "broke something" because they have gone "every Sunday since before the kids were born," marking a break from an important family ritual.
rationales.benevolence: She knows her cancellation hurts her parents, hearing her father "putting the plates away," yet still withdraws from showing up for close family that day.

rationales.tradition: She insists on weekly visits because "it's just what we do" and refuses to be the one who lets the family table shrink, actively preserving an inherited pattern.
rationales.benevolence: She keeps making the difficult trip because otherwise "My parents eat alone," prioritizing the emotional wellbeing of close family over convenience.

rationales.tradition: She treasures the gratin made "exactly how Grand-mère taught Maman, how Maman taught me" and sees the recipe box as a way of passing down a whole inherited way of life.
rationales.benevolence: She involves Léa in stirring the béchamel and experiences that small act as "exactly right," showing care through feeding and teaching her child.

rationales.tradition: The whole entry centers on repeating family rituals and stories, ending with "that's what we do," which affirms continuity across generations.
rationales.benevolence: She values gathering the family, notices Hugo leaning into his grandfather's story, and wants to keep these bonds and memories alive for her children and parents.

rationales.self_direction: She worries that staying home may be "just fear of doing something different from what I know," suggesting reluctance to choose a path independent of inherited expectations.
rationales.tradition: She explicitly measures herself against what "Maman, and Grand-mère before her" did, treating their model of motherhood and home life as the standard to preserve.
rationales.benevolence: Her reasoning centers on being there when the children come home, cooking for them, knowing their teachers, and volunteering at school, all focused on caring for close family.

rationales.hedonism: She plans to take the time from "sleep" or from "the one evening a week I watch something for myself," sacrificing personal rest and enjoyment.
rationales.tradition: She volunteers because the procession is something her parents did and she wants to keep the lanterns from disappearing, actively preserving a communal custom.
rationales.benevolence: She would "rather be tired than explain to Hugo why the lanterns aren't happening anymore," showing willingness to bear the cost for her child's sake.

rationales.achievement: She describes "four months of work" ending with pieces "looking exactly the way I wanted them to look," showing pride in completing demanding work to her standards.
rationales.security: Her immediate relief was that "the gallery check will clear next week," tying the successful batch to financial stability and predictability.

rationales.self_direction: She insists "I do have a plan" built from contracts, workshops, and commissions, defending a self-authored path even when her family does not recognize it.

rationales.security: She is doing inventory, ordering supplies for "the next quarter," and noting booked workshops "through August," all of which emphasize maintaining financial and operational stability.

rationales.self_direction: She frames the compromise as "a trade I chose and I would choose it again," explicitly affirming autonomy over her work mix.
rationales.security: The entry is about managing "what I want to make" against "what I need to sell," showing ongoing attention to keeping the practice financially viable.

rationales.self_direction: She accepts the Beirut show because the pieces were "made for myself, not on commission" and the chance for someone to connect with them matters more than easier options.
rationales.security: She says yes despite "no artist fee" and an expected "logistics headache," choosing artistic exposure over the safer financial choice.
Signing a "six-month lease renewal" at the same rate makes the studio arrangement more fixed and predictable, which directly supports stability.

rationales.self_direction: She saves the accidental residue in a jar and decides "I will try to figure out what happened," pursuing her own experimental solution rather than letting it go.
rationales.achievement: Her frustration comes from "months" of testing, logs, and ratios in pursuit of a specific effect, showing disciplined striving for technical mastery.
She carefully protects and advances her work by building a crate herself and continuing glaze experiments the same day, reflecting serious investment in craft and output.
rationales.security: She packs the vessels in "three layers" and says "I am not trusting them to someone else's packing job," prioritizing protection against loss or damage.
She tracks "every fils" so she can honestly say "everything is fine" and sleep at night, showing a strong need for dependable financial footing.
rationales.benevolence: She wires her mother money "not a lot, but enough that she will not have to ask again for a while," directly supporting a close family member.

rationales.self_direction: She chooses the extra workshop because "at least it is mine — my studio, my schedule, my terms," preferring autonomy over better-paid outside direction.
rationales.security: The whole decision is driven by a thinner buffer and making sure "the math still works," which shows active management of financial stability.

rationales.hedonism: He refuses to "stop living just because the spreadsheet says I should" and keeps the expensive parrilla outing because of the pleasure of "good food and good talk."
rationales.security: He is calculating whether he can "cover everything," preserving money for insurance, and posting "DO NOT touch savings," all of which center on financial safety.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects going back to "some warehouse" and says "I just don't want to," defending an independent way of working.
rationales.security: He dismisses the more stable regular-job path and contrasts himself with Graciela's line-item life, showing resistance to predictability and institutional safety.
rationales.conformity: He openly refuses the family expectation that he should return to a conventional job, resisting the pressure to fit a more socially approved path.

rationales.self_direction: Even while doubting the trade-off, he refuses to "crawl back and prove Graciela right," holding onto autonomy rather than submitting to a boss.
rationales.security: He describes income as "a guess" and worries he does not know what he is going to make each week, highlighting instability and threat to basic security.
A "good-sized job" makes him feel he can "breathe again," showing that predictable paid work restores a sense of stability.
The broken motorcycle threatens deliveries, rent, and emergency reserves, and his distress centers on having "nothing between me and the next bad surprise."

rationales.hedonism: Instead of resting, he takes delivery runs at 1am and comes home at 3am, sacrificing comfort and immediate wellbeing for cash.
rationales.achievement: He actively hustles for extra work in the middle of the night, showing strong effort to get the job done despite exhaustion.
rationales.security: He takes the runs because he needs "barely enough to cover tomorrow's gas," acting to shore up immediate financial survival.

rationales.hedonism: He defends the parrilla because it is "the one thing that keeps me sane" and the only time he is "just Roberto having a good time with his friends."
rationales.security: He is sleep-deprived, nearly clips a bus, and still prioritizes the costly outing over saving, which undercuts caution and stability.

rationales.hedonism: He immediately celebrates the new painting job by buying Negrita "the fancy wet food she likes," using money for a small pleasure.
rationales.security: The appeal of the job is also that it is "good rate" work with "half upfront," which improves short-term financial stability.

rationales.self_direction: He says he likes "picking my own hours" and riding around with "nobody telling me where to be," clearly valuing autonomy in daily life.
rationales.security: He admits he "left a stable situation because of pride" and is frightened by how little order and maintenance he has preserved in his own life.

rationales.hedonism: He keeps going to the parrilla because it is "the part of my life that doesn't feel like survival," prioritizing enjoyment and relief in the present.
rationales.security: Even with only a "small cushion," he worries the enjoyment "might not last" and thinks he should be saving instead, showing conflict with financial stability.

rationales.hedonism: He chooses to bring "the good Malbec" because "sometimes you just want to do something a little extra for no reason," emphasizing present enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: He fixes the chair and patches the paint as soon as he gets going, taking concrete action to improve neglected problems.
rationales.security: He explicitly notes that "next week is uncertain" but takes satisfaction in having the chair fixed, the wall clean, and some money still in hand, restoring a sense of order and steadiness.

rationales.hedonism: He lingers over a pleasurable family moment, "sat on the balcony eating tamales" and noticing the jalapeno shoots, treating simple enjoyment as meaningful.
rationales.achievement: "Delivered the payment gateway migration three days early" shows clear competence and strong performance against work standards.

rationales.self_direction: He suppresses his own informed judgment even after "I ran the numbers over the weekend," deciding not to voice it because the architect already endorsed the plan.
rationales.security: He chooses the safer path by documenting concerns for a "paper trail" so he is "covered without making it a thing," prioritizing protection over confrontation.
rationales.conformity: He restrains himself because speaking up would "make things awkward" and avoids challenging a known social hierarchy in the standup.

rationales.achievement: He inventories concrete accomplishments like "seventeen production releases," "zero rollbacks," and mentoring juniors, then commits to quantifying them for the review.
rationales.conformity: Even though self-promotion feels like "wearing a costume," he decides to "use the strong verbs" because that is what the system expects.

rationales.achievement: He recognizes the scale of his contributions, from a six-month migration to shaping junior engineers, and turns that into "four paragraphs" of substantive accomplishment.
rationales.conformity: He accepts that "the rules here are different" and adapts to that professional norm by writing in the "new professional language" his manager can evaluate.

rationales.security: His focus is on uncertainty and downside management, framing the meeting as something that "could be good" or could be unwanted, and refusing to get "set up for another no."

rationales.self_direction: He concludes the promoted version of himself is still authentic, saying "I wasn't lying. I was translating," which affirms agency over how he presents himself.
rationales.hedonism: He marks the good news by taking "the girls out for helado to celebrate," choosing a simple present pleasure.
rationales.achievement: Being put forward for the director role and hearing his self-assessment was "the strongest you've written" reflects clear advancement and recognized competence.
He submits a full director application packet, including a restructuring proposal, which is a direct bid to demonstrate leadership and competence.
rationales.conformity: He deliberately keeps the proposal "diplomatic" because he knows "bold doesn't play well with everyone" on the review panel, adapting to audience expectations.

rationales.achievement: He treats the panel interview seriously, saying "I said what I prepared" and answered hard questions in a disciplined effort to perform well.
rationales.conformity: "Didn't improvise, didn't overshoot" and emphasizing that he "defaults to listening first" show careful self-restraint to meet formal interview expectations.

rationales.power: She asserts authority by telling Andres "one more time and I'm finding someone else" and stating "the gallery is mine and the standard is mine to set."
rationales.security: Her boundary is driven by protecting the business from instability, noting she "can't have my space looking disorganized when there's money on the table."

rationales.achievement: She experiences the finished triptych as a success, saying "what I made matches what I saw in my head," which signals high standards met.
rationales.security: She emphasizes paying rent on time, having "the next three months covered," and tracking when the commission check clears, all centered on stability and financial assurance.

rationales.power: She lets the potential co-investor dominate the meeting, admitting he "kept talking over me" and that she "smiled and nodded" instead of asserting her position.
rationales.conformity: By "smil[ing] and nod[ding] through most of it instead of pushing back," she maintains surface harmony rather than risking open conflict.

rationales.self_direction: She values making the gallery fully her own, recalling that under the shared lease "nothing felt fully mine" and now every corner reflects "a decision I made."
rationales.power: Her attention goes to who "commands a space," and she ties the gallery's strength to having sole control over the room and its presentation.

rationales.security: Even on a day off she reviews invoices because she likes knowing "exactly where the money sits, what's coming in, what's going out," showing a strong need for financial predictability.

rationales.achievement: She describes curating the March lineup as skilled orchestration, taking a "calculated" risk on a new artist while building a strong exhibition.
rationales.power: She explicitly embraces deciding "whose career gets a platform and whose doesn't," taking satisfaction in having that gatekeeping authority.
rationales.security: She underscores that "the reserve fund is untouched" and "insurance renewal is handled," because solid finances let everything else feel stable.

rationales.self_direction: She refuses to accept a role definition imposed on her, writing back to "clarify terms before anything moves forward" rather than letting someone else set the path.
rationales.power: The core issue is control over status and authority: she reacts strongly to someone trying to "slide into a title I haven't offered" and says "the boundary is drawn."

rationales.achievement: She takes satisfaction in execution before the opening, noting the room is ready and everything is in place for the show.
rationales.power: "Everything is exactly where I placed it" highlights her control over the space and over how others will encounter the work.

rationales.achievement: Selling "four pieces in the first two hours" and seeing the risky new artist sell confirm successful performance and reputation-building.
rationales.power: A collector saying he'd "been hearing my name" and her focus on people knowing "the name Villalobos" show clear concern with status and influence in the scene.
rationales.security: She reassures her mother that the gallery is not a "reckless bet anymore" and stresses that "the reserve is intact," centering financial stability.

rationales.self_direction: She defends her independent path, noting she "left a stable partnership to build this alone" and refuses to let a stranger define her choices.
rationales.power: "My gallery, my choices, my track record" is an explicit assertion of ownership, authority, and control over her work.
rationales.security: She justifies her approach by "keeping my lights on and my artists paid," prioritizing practical stability alongside artistic decisions.

rationales.power: She draws a hard boundary around authority, saying of artist selection, "I won't give that up," even while negotiating for expansion capital.
rationales.security: Her dilemma is shaped by financial constraints and risk, since she "can't fund the expansion alone" and "the bank terms are worse," making stability a central concern.

rationales.hedonism: He ended the day realizing he "hadn't eaten since a cheese sandwich at eleven" and that even his plants "aren't getting my best," showing active self-denial rather than present comfort.
rationales.benevolence: He repeatedly prioritized his sister and her children, from picking the kids up and buying them stroopwafels to making Lotte tea and putting on washing before tending to himself.
He gave up his Sunday morning, cycled over with tools, fixed the leak, and stayed with Lotte while she cried, showing concrete care for close family despite the personal cost.
He spent the afternoon playing a board game with the kids and chose to "carry it" rather than say something that would confirm Lotte's fear, prioritizing her emotional wellbeing over his own relief.

rationales.achievement: He notes a "full delivery day for once" with "eight runs between nine and four" and tracks that the earnings were "decent," showing clear focus on work performance and output.
rationales.benevolence: Even while busy, he feels guilty for refusing coffee to a lonely older woman and resolves to show up for Jeroen because "Jeroen always shows up for me," signaling active concern for people close to him.

rationales.security: He is confronted with "the next few years" of increasing caregiving and says the burden "keeps getting heavier," highlighting destabilizing uncertainty rather than safety or predictability.
rationales.benevolence: He frames stepping back as impossible because "the gap isn't filled by someone else," and keeps showing up for Lotte despite fear and exhaustion, centering a close family member's needs.

rationales.hedonism: He "drank too many Jupilers," "talked rubbish," and "laughed until my stomach hurt," clearly embracing immediate enjoyment and relief.
rationales.benevolence: He followed through on going to Jeroen's birthday and was moved when his friend said he's glad he came, showing investment in an important close relationship.

rationales.self_direction: He explicitly rejects his mother's script to "focus on your own life," insisting "I'm not doing this out of obligation" and defining the choice on his own terms.
rationales.security: He describes "the kind where you wake up already behind" and mornings when he sits on the bed before standing up, showing chronic strain and depleted stability.
rationales.benevolence: He says helping Lotte "IS my life" and that he'd "rather be tired than be the kind of person who steps away when things get hard," making sustained care for family the guiding choice.

rationales.universalism: Seeing "a child no older than ten carrying bricks" makes him dwell on the moral gap between his work and broader human suffering, showing concern for injustice beyond his immediate circle.
He reflects that he has had "the privilege to forget" what survival looks like, widening the entry from one child to a broader awareness of hardship and inequality.

rationales.self_direction: He independently chose to enroll in "an online course on building accessible software for low-resource settings," redirecting his expertise toward a self-authored purpose.
