rationales.security: Leaving a doctoral program for an overseas startup is a direct move away from a more predictable path.
rationales.conformity: By accepting, he commits to a choice he has repeatedly framed as something others in his program and family may not approve of.

rationales.self_direction: He follows through with telling Sharma and his parents and keeps moving toward the path he chose rather than retreating.
rationales.stimulation: He describes the 'fizzy feeling' of 'something NEW coming' and sees even the worst case as a year in Singapore learning new things.
rationales.security: He knows this feeling has preceded past upheavals and still proceeds despite fearing he may get 'stuck again' and need 'the next exit.'

rationales.self_direction: He accepts being 'the one who's walking away' from the PhD instead of staying committed to an old promise from undergrad.
rationales.security: Packing to leave the program for a new country reflects a move away from the more stable path his earlier self imagined when he wrote 'I will definitely finish my PhD.'

rationales.self_direction: He says 'at some point you just have to move' and insists he needs to make 'A choice' rather than remain trapped by others' questions.
rationales.stimulation: He embraces Singapore because 'at least it'll be something different,' explicitly valuing change over the current path.
rationales.security: He rejects the PhD as 'safety' and chooses an uncertain future he cannot verify in advance.
rationales.conformity: He persists despite being repeatedly asked if he is 'sure,' after already questioning whether he is 'disappointing everyone.'

rationales.self_direction: She deliberately 'turned my phone off' and protected time where 'nobody needed anything from me,' asserting control over her day.
rationales.hedonism: She cooks shakshuka, drinks coffee, reads slowly, and says 'I needed it. My body needed it,' clearly prioritizing restorative pleasure and comfort.
Even with wine and music, she keeps calculating work and finally opens the laptop, saying she felt relieved to stop 'pretending I was relaxing.'
rationales.achievement: She returns to work for another two hours after already leaving early, showing strong pull toward productivity over rest.
rationales.benevolence: She justifies reopening the laptop because 'someone on my team might be stuck,' showing concern for close coworkers' needs.

rationales.hedonism: After a ten-hour day she wants to 'run a bath' but instead opens the laptop because she cannot even tell herself she 'deserved an hour off.'
rationales.achievement: She sacrifices recovery time to prep for tomorrow's review, prioritizing performance after an already exhausting workday.

rationales.self_direction: She chooses to leave the laptop behind and sit quietly at the coffee shop, acting on what she wants instead of defaulting to work.
rationales.hedonism: She lingers over a good cortado in 'low and gold' light and describes the pause as 'genuinely nice,' embracing a small pleasure for its own sake.
She goes to a restaurant she'd wanted to try, but spends 'half the meal thinking about the quarterly review deck' and leaves feeling absent from an enjoyable experience.
rationales.achievement: Work obligations dominate even during dinner with a friend, showing productivity demands overriding leisure.

rationales.security: She carefully manages each conference so there will be 'No difficult calls tomorrow,' prioritizing predictability and avoiding conflict escalation.
rationales.conformity: She performs the 'careful dance' of saying 'all the right things' and keeping the proper expression all day to meet institutional and social expectations.
rationales.benevolence: She chooses tiring diplomacy because she'd 'rather be tired than cause that kind of mess,' showing care for families and colleagues' relationships.

rationales.security: She follows protocol 'exactly' and emphasizes that looking away would mean acting as if 'rules don't matter,' prioritizing order and institutional safety.
rationales.conformity: She acts 'by the book' despite emotional discomfort and repeatedly frames the report as what was 'expected' and proper.
rationales.tradition: She leans on her mother's example that 'A teacher who ignores cheating isn't a teacher,' grounding the decision in inherited professional norms.
rationales.benevolence: She knows reporting Jia-Hui cost the student's trust and turns herself into 'the teacher who got her in trouble' rather than the supportive figure she wants to be.

rationales.achievement: She tells her mother she is 'focused on work right now' and frames exam period as too exhausting to think about anything else, prioritizing task completion.

rationales.self_direction: She admits 'most things I do are because someone is expecting me to do them' and that she has 'started to forget what I want when no one's watching.'
rationales.hedonism: She gives up a ceramics class she wants and settles for 'watching YouTube videos about ceramics instead,' denying herself a pleasurable activity.
rationales.conformity: She is the 'voice of the department' because she 'doesn't argue in meetings' and keeps smoothing interactions with polite restraint.
rationales.tradition: She lets family routine govern her choice, saying 'That's not how my family works. We show up for each other. That's the deal.'
rationales.benevolence: She does not sign up because it conflicts with her parents' usual visiting time and she prioritizes showing up for family over her own hobby.

rationales.self_direction: She keeps the registration form untouched and tells herself 'There's nothing wrong with my life the way it is,' suppressing the personal desire she keeps returning to.
rationales.hedonism: She again postpones the ceramics class she wants, letting worries about cost, grading, and family discussion override enjoyment.
rationales.security: She emphasizes having a 'stable job,' 'parents who care,' and 'a routine that works,' showing attachment to stability over experimentation.
rationales.conformity: At the dinner she behaves exactly as expected, smiling politely and noting the evening was 'exactly what it was supposed to be.'
rationales.benevolence: She centers 'Sunday dinners are important. Family is important,' prioritizing family obligations over her own leisure.

rationales.self_direction: He frames the company as something he built to do things "my way" and keeps returning to the importance of making his own decisions.
rationales.benevolence: He "held back" and let Anna present because her softer version was right for the client, prioritizing a colleague and the team outcome over his own ego.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects "standard recommendations for standard companies" and fixates on making "actual decisions that affect actual outcomes," showing a strong pull toward acting on his own terms.
rationales.conformity: He dismisses the association's guidance and "left early," actively resisting the expected norms of the meeting.

rationales.self_direction: He cannot "stomach the idea of someone else making decisions about my work again" and defines the real loss as giving up the ability to choose his own path.
rationales.power: What matters to him is "walking into a room and knowing that whatever happens next, I chose it," which centers control over his work and circumstances.
rationales.security: He explicitly weighs and resists "stability," "predictability," and "a good pension," even after a rough year for the consultancy.

rationales.self_direction: He closes by grounding himself in the fact that the consultancy is "still mine," emphasizing ownership of his work and choices.
rationales.conformity: He restrains his first instinct to "push back hard," lets Erik handle the diplomacy, and focuses on keeping the client and team aligned.
rationales.tradition: He agrees to attend the "Lucia dinner" and treats the seasonal observance as something worth showing up for.
rationales.benevolence: He chooses the path where the "client's happy, team's intact," showing clear concern for preserving relationships around him.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects Marcus's offer because what he wants is "to keep making choices" and to face problems that are "problems I chose."
rationales.security: He accepts that "she wanted more certainty than I could give her" and embraces a path where the rough years and costs are still worth it.
rationales.benevolence: He closes the office by handing out bonuses and lingers on the fact that "the team that stays anyway is mine," signaling real care for his people.

rationales.power: She lets someone present "my stakeholder mapping framework" as "something I've been working on" without asserting ownership, surrendering status and authorship in the room.
rationales.conformity: She "sat there and said nothing" despite the appropriation, choosing restraint over challenging the seminar's social dynamics.

rationales.hedonism: She calls the coffee conversation with Astrid "the best part of the week," valuing uncomplicated enjoyment amid the grind.
rationales.benevolence: What she treasures is that "nobody's positioning themselves" and they can just talk and complain together, which reflects warmth toward a close collegial relationship.

rationales.hedonism: She sacrifices basic comfort, noting "my eyes are burning" and that she has not cooked in days while pushing through the work.
rationales.achievement: She is focused on finishing the chapter draft and reshaping it for "the journal we're targeting," showing strong investment in performance and external success.
rationales.conformity: She accepts that "sometimes you have to write for the audience that matters," adapting her work to reviewer expectations rather than insisting on her preferred framing.

rationales.achievement: She is explicitly optimizing for "a better review at the European Journal of Political Economy," treating publication success as worth strategic compromise.
rationales.power: She sees "the game" clearly, leverages her supervisor's name to open doors, and withholds information because people are "vying for the same limited spots."
rationales.benevolence: She admits she "didn't" tell Erik everything she knew because of the competition, choosing self-advantage over helping a peer fully.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects becoming "just another doctoral student waiting for permission" and insists that if she stops pushing, she gives up agency.
rationales.achievement: She prepares a proper proposal with references and an implementation plan, then says she will "do it again," showing persistent drive to perform effectively.
rationales.power: She frames the problem as needing "a different kind of leverage" and identifies herself as someone who has spent too long "making things happen" to accept powerlessness.

rationales.self_direction: She cannot tolerate being stuck on someone else's reply and immediately thinks, "I'd find another route. There's always another route."
rationales.achievement: She has the visiting researcher application fully prepared and keeps hunting for a way to submit it on time, reflecting strong ambition and goal pursuit.

rationales.self_direction: She refuses to "pretend I don't know how to organise people or push things forward" and resists shrinking herself to fit doctoral-student expectations.
rationales.power: She embraces directing meetings, drafting agendas, and following up action items, presenting control and influence as part of doing things properly.
rationales.conformity: She openly takes up "more space than the average doctoral student" and pushes against others' expectations instead of adapting to them.

rationales.achievement: Her focus stays on which case studies will make the report stronger, and she immediately identifies that Lena's choices create "gaps" and a weaker outcome for the partner.

rationales.universalism: He worries that cutting costs on filters used by people who "cannot complain if it breaks" would harm vulnerable communities, centering their welfare over procurement convenience.

rationales.security: Even though the Dubai salary would cover "both girls' tuition in one year" and bring obvious relief, he keeps choosing the more precarious path and lets the safer option sit unresolved.
rationales.universalism: What keeps him from taking the simpler job is commitment to work where "clean water matters" and families are still drinking from poorly serviced tanks.

rationales.achievement: He personally "tested every seal" and tracks the factory output closely, showing pride in competent execution and product quality.
rationales.universalism: The entry ends on Abu Faris saying the filter meant "the kids stopped getting sick last winter," grounding the work in broader human wellbeing.

rationales.achievement: He wants ideas to be "bulletproof" before presenting them, showing a strong concern with competence and not being caught unprepared again.
rationales.security: After being embarrassed once, he now avoids risk by keeping ideas to himself until he has "run the numbers," favoring caution and predictability.

rationales.universalism: He cannot stop doing "math in my head about how many households that affects" when projects are suspended, showing deep concern for people far beyond his immediate circle.

He accepts the deal because "Fifty units is fifty families with clean water," yet recoils at treating camp residents as "props," showing strong concern for both welfare and dignity beyond his own circle.

rationales.security: He admits he does not know whether the work will "outlast the next funding cycle" and feels that uncertainty is "eating through the floor under me," signaling a life marked by instability rather than safety.
rationales.universalism: He explains the business in terms of camps and how water-borne illness keeps kids out of school, then insists that "helping for a while still matters," centering broader human need.

rationales.achievement: Despite the jammed press and exhaustion, he makes sure "the Zarqa order" is "all sealed and loaded," emphasizing getting the job done well and on time.
rationales.benevolence: He notices that Abu Faris brought knafeh "to share with the crew" and values how it made the overtime feel less grim, highlighting care within his immediate work circle.

rationales.power: He explicitly rejects status markers like "Cars, titles, where your kids go to school" and reduces Samir's promotion to "a line on a business card."
rationales.universalism: He grounds his sense of what is "real" in a mother saying her son's eczema cleared after the filter installation, prioritizing tangible human welfare over prestige.

rationales.hedonism: He keeps sacrificing comfort and enjoyment, eating "lunch... at my desk again" and abandoning the balcony because "once I picked up the phone it was over."
rationales.achievement: He structures the day around duties and follow-through, from finalizing the roster to updating projections and reviewing "tomorrow's agenda" at home.
rationales.benevolence: He approved a worker's request for "the entire last week of December off" because "he hasn't taken time in months," prioritizing that person's needs.

rationales.hedonism: He tries to enjoy a quiet tea on the balcony but says he "couldn't let myself have it for more than five minutes" before turning it into another problem.
rationales.achievement: Even at "eleven at night," he measures himself against being productive and feels unable to sit still without justification.

rationales.hedonism: The abandoned pleasure is the whole point here: he finds the tea "cold on the railing" after repeatedly failing to actually enjoy it.
He deliberately savors a small pleasure, buying "knafeh on the way home," eating it "still warm," and not checking his phone at all.
He denies himself rest despite being "genuinely tired," stays for emails that "could have waited," and says guilt is "louder than the tiredness."
rationales.conformity: He stays because of "how that would look" if he left at three while the team stayed until five, showing strong pressure from role expectations.
rationales.benevolence: He frames his own rest as different because "if they rest, I'm the one who covered," centering responsibility for protecting his team.

rationales.hedonism: A gathering with "good food and music" becomes joyless because he can't stop thinking about work and leaves early.
rationales.achievement: A looming "procurement deadline on Monday" dominates his attention so completely that it overrides the event he had "looked forward to all week."

rationales.self_direction: The decisive tension is that the mural brief feels like "their story, not mine," and the entry centers wanting to author work on their own terms.
rationales.security: They say they "should probably say yes because I need the money" but feel a tightness in their chest about taking the safer paid option.
rationales.universalism: The family from Syria and the girl's drawings reshape the whole day, and the artist keeps returning to whether the work serves something larger than branding.

rationales.self_direction: They turn down the commission, feel "RELIEVED," and commit to making something real rather than accepting a brief that does not fit their own direction.
rationales.stimulation: The artist is pulled toward a new medium that could "move" and says static murals were "starting to feel like cages," signaling active pursuit of novelty and change.
rationales.achievement: They reject the recognizable paid project and say they'd "rather be broke and making something real than comfortable and decorating," stepping away from conventional success markers.
rationales.security: Choosing "no money in it probably" over the safer commission openly sacrifices stability for conviction.
rationales.conformity: They refuse the expected commercial path and reject simply delivering what clients or the art world would find easy to accept.
rationales.universalism: The proposed next project centers the refugee kids so it can tell "THEIR story instead of a brand's story," driven by concern beyond the self.

rationales.self_direction: He chooses the "very safe" hotel job while saying the honest series is work he "can't show to anyone," suppressing his own artistic path.
rationales.conformity: He explicitly orients around anticipated family reactions like his uncle asking why the work is "so depressing," and defaults to the option others will accept.

rationales.self_direction: He hides a real relationship and gives only "the correct answers," showing he is not acting on what he actually wants to say or disclose.
rationales.conformity: The whole lunch is managed through deflection to avoid upsetting family and community expectations, and he describes himself as efficient at predicting which battles not to start.

rationales.self_direction: He admits the hidden series is "the best work I've ever done" yet still says no to showing it, choosing avoidance over his own artistic voice.
rationales.stimulation: He keeps making work he calls "technically proficient and completely forgettable" instead of pursuing the more challenging, boundary-pushing show.
rationales.conformity: He names the real obstacle as disappointing people and drawing attention he is "not ready for," so he sticks with what keeps things socially smooth.
He describes the engagement party script almost mechanically: "I'll wear the right shirt, say the right things, congratulate the right people," emphasizing compliance with expectations.
He "smiled through all of it" at the party and feels relief when his aunt calls him "a good boy," showing how strongly he is organized around approval and proper behavior.

rationales.self_direction: He admits he could make time for the show but will not because he does not want to reveal work that "actually says something," holding back his own expression.
rationales.conformity: He explicitly chooses to "keep things smooth" and preserve being "predictable" in the eyes of family rather than disrupt that social image.

rationales.self_direction: He nearly posts the work he knows is good, then imagines family reactions and "covered everything back up," retreating from his own artistic choice.
rationales.conformity: What stops him is fear of losing "the approval, the ease, the feeling of fitting in," which he treats as foundational.

rationales.self_direction: His authentic imagery emerges in the hotel piece, but he immediately "painted over them before anyone could see," undoing his own impulse.
rationales.conformity: Even after his sister shows interest, he still tells her the hidden series is "nothing, just experiments," keeping the family-facing version of himself intact.

rationales.self_direction: The praise hurts because the finished piece was "exactly what he envisioned," meaning the artist delivered someone else's vision rather than his own.
rationales.conformity: The commission succeeds precisely by matching the client's expectations, and his depressed reaction highlights how much he has been accommodating external demands.

rationales.self_direction: He finally shows his sister the hidden pieces and admits he may be "getting tired of hiding the work," taking a concrete step toward owning what he actually wants to make.
rationales.conformity: Leaving the work uncovered and noticing that "the tarp felt heavier putting it back on" signals movement away from the old habit of concealment for others' comfort.

rationales.hedonism: She "stayed past six" and frames doing "one more review session" as more important than rest or comfort, showing self-denial in the present.
rationales.achievement: She systematically tracks who is falling behind and takes pride that reading club students are now "a full grade level above," showing strong focus on measurable performance.
rationales.benevolence: She feels personally responsible if "Ravi in 8B fails" and cannot separate herself from her students' outcomes, showing deep care for specific people close to her work.
rationales.universalism: She worries students will stay in "the same cycle their parents were stuck in," expressing concern about broader inequality beyond individual grades.

rationales.hedonism: She "came in on Saturdays" and dismisses feeling proud because she is fixated on the gap, putting enjoyment and ease behind performance pressure.
rationales.achievement: She wants "First. Or at least second" and details how she mapped weak areas and differentiated worksheets, showing intense drive to excel against external benchmarks.

rationales.hedonism: She spent "two hours building supplementary inference exercises tonight instead of eating dinner," explicitly sacrificing immediate comfort.
rationales.achievement: Even while upset about rankings, she analyzes the breakdown by question type and builds new exercises to improve results, showing persistent performance focus.
rationales.universalism: She argues the test reflects "privilege" and disadvantages students whose home language differs from the test, showing concern about structural inequality.

rationales.self_direction: After official delay, she focuses on what she can "actually push on" and considers going to the press, showing independent action rather than passive compliance.
rationales.benevolence: She is distressed that "Lakshmi in 7A" may be affected and cannot stop thinking about students drinking unsafe water, showing protective care toward people close to her.
rationales.universalism: She gathers six months of water data and is prepared to pressure authorities over contamination affecting the school community, showing concern for public welfare and health.

rationales.hedonism: She says the missing and comfort of home feel like "something I can't afford right now," choosing work over rest, family warmth, and personal ease.
rationales.achievement: Even on Sunday she is working through lesson materials and a self-evaluation, and she judges herself in terms of effort and results, showing strong performance orientation.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects a colleague's suggestion to "let the NGO stuff go" and independently sets a boundary of fieldwork "every other Saturday," showing self-authored choices on both commitment and limits.
rationales.hedonism: She notices that cutting back let her get through the week without "Sunday-night dread" and says "that's worth something," giving real weight to her own relief.
rationales.universalism: She refuses to pause the NGO work because that would mean "those water readings sit in a folder and nothing happens," showing commitment to a wider public-health issue.

rationales.hedonism: She "slept maybe four hours" and worked until "2 AM," explicitly trading away rest to keep refining the lesson.
rationales.achievement: She rewrote passages, tested them, and takes satisfaction in the immediate improvement, showing disciplined effort to make the material work better.
rationales.universalism: She changes culturally specific passages to contexts her students "would actually recognize," addressing unfair barriers tied to class and cultural background.

rationales.achievement: She tracks that the pass rate rose from "38% to 61%" and carefully documents the evidence, showing strong focus on demonstrable improvement.
rationales.universalism: She pursues both the water-case escalation and evidence that the standardized test is biased, showing concern with public accountability and fairness beyond her own standing.

rationales.achievement: She says she "genuinely cannot take three days off right now" because she must prep students for boards, putting work performance first.
rationales.tradition: She is missing "Pongal" despite its family significance, so her choices are actively pulling her away from an important cultural observance.
rationales.benevolence: Her decision leaves her mother hurt and "quiet," showing that work demands are crowding out responsiveness to a close family relationship.

rationales.hedonism: She says "the water report matters more than my comfort" and her students matter more than her "sleep schedule," explicitly subordinating pleasure and rest.
rationales.achievement: She submits an "Emerging Educator portfolio" and adds a formal note arguing for contextual performance measures, showing sustained investment in recognized competence and results.
rationales.benevolence: She centers "my students' futures" and feels depleted because of how much she gives to them, showing deep commitment to the people directly under her care.
rationales.universalism: She spent "six months of volunteer hours" pushing the water issue and argues against benchmarks that ignore context, showing concern for systemic fairness and public wellbeing.

rationales.security: He refuses to "just wait and see" and immediately runs the numbers on the pay change to protect his income and plan for possible loss.
He admits he "broke my own system trying to grab a little more" and disrupted a routine that normally keeps work predictable and manageable.
He deletes the extra app, returns to "the two I know," logs earnings, and plans routine bike maintenance, all aimed at keeping work stable and under control.
He knowingly rides with brakes "grinding metal on metal" and keeps postponing repair for "one more day," choosing short-term earnings over basic safety and stability.
He finally fixes the brakes and emphasizes that the bike now "stops clean" and he feels "better on the road," showing restored priority on safety and reliability.
He refuses to risk "two months of savings" on a shaky food truck idea and insists "the money stays where it is," prioritizing financial protection over speculative upside.
He abandons his known route for a shortcut, gets a one-star rating, and now risks lower queue priority and income, undermining the predictability he depends on.
He transfers the streak bonus to savings "immediately" and tracks whether he can cover June insurance without dipping into reserves, showing deliberate effort to preserve financial stability.

He pays bike insurance early, values having "one less thing on the list," and plans the plumbing repair around savings and obligations, showing strong preference for order, protection, and future stability.

rationales.tradition: She made the kheer "exactly how Nani used to make it" and linked the process to family memory and Diwali, showing active preservation of inherited practices.
rationales.benevolence: She centered Amma's birthday, called her afterward, and described helping drape her sari as "our thing," emphasizing care for close family.

rationales.hedonism: She keeps taking double shifts until "hour fourteen," skips proper meals, and uses work instead of rest or comfort, actively sacrificing present enjoyment.
rationales.security: She works extra because "my parents' medical bills keep growing" and she is saving for "Meera's wedding fund," prioritizing financial stability and family protection.
rationales.tradition: "This is what daughters do" frames her choices as fulfilling an inherited family duty rather than a purely personal preference.
rationales.benevolence: Her overwork is directed toward supporting close relatives, from her parents' bills to her brother's struggling shop and Meera's future wedding costs.

rationales.self_direction: She defends Meera's right to choose her own path, concluding that "Meera chose it. That matters more than a wedding date."
rationales.conformity: She "just smiled and nodded because that is what you do," deliberately restraining herself to keep the social interaction smooth.
rationales.tradition: She explicitly favors Meera's self-chosen career over the marriage expectations voiced by Sunita auntie, pulling away from the customary script.

rationales.hedonism: She is so depleted by "back-to-back shifts" and four hours of sleep that she turns down the walk and can barely engage with dinner or television, showing active loss of pleasure and comfort.

rationales.tradition: She "skipped the temple" and avoided the puja despite knowing its importance, which is a clear break from a communal religious practice.

rationales.benevolence: She spent her break sitting with Mrs. Okonkwo, held her hand, and brought her chai so the patient would feel less alone.
rationales.universalism: She empathizes across cultural difference, noting that they are "not so different" and responding to the needs of someone outside her own circle with humane concern.

rationales.tradition: She measures the situation against a family pattern where "all the women in our family stayed close," showing strong attachment to inherited continuity.
rationales.benevolence: Her distress centers on losing everyday closeness with Meera, from dinner talk to being within "come over for chai" distance.

rationales.tradition: Teaching Meera the special-occasion dhokla recipe and retelling stories about Nani shows deliberate transmission of family heritage.
rationales.benevolence: She invests two hours cooking with Meera and sharing intimate family stories so her daughter can carry them forward.

rationales.tradition: "Three generations passing the same batter between their hands" frames the recipe as a living family inheritance being maintained across generations.
rationales.benevolence: She sends the recipe onward and treats the handoff to her mother and daughter as an act of family care and connection.

rationales.stimulation: They are "crawling out of my skin," refreshing job boards, and wanting to "book a flight somewhere, anywhere," showing strong aversion to boredom and routine.
"Sitting around feels like drowning" and the immediate leap at a new responsibility show a strong need for activity and momentum.
rationales.power: "The moment she said 'lead' I was already in" and "now I have something with my name on it" show clear attraction to authority and ownership.

rationales.achievement: They insist "I fixed that" about the missed shipments and care deeply that capability and initiative be recognized.
rationales.power: "It's my committee" and the frustration at being undermined by senior colleagues show a strong drive to retain authority and control.
rationales.conformity: They reject being put in a deferential "you're still new here" role and bristle at the expectation to accept seniority-based restraint.

rationales.stimulation: They describe moving fast because "slow feels unbearable" and say "Sometimes I just need to move to think," showing a clear pull toward motion and change.
rationales.hedonism: The walk by the harbor, taking photos "for the gram," and feeling restored by the sunset reflect active pursuit of enjoyable sensory experience.
rationales.conformity: They reconsidered the conflict, acknowledged others "needed to feel heard," and reached out to Ms. Liu to collaborate rather than keep pushing solo.

rationales.power: She is most upset that Amna took public ownership of her work and that "I let her," showing a failure to assert control over status and credit in the moment.
rationales.conformity: She stayed silent because calling it out "in front of the whole team" would look petty, prioritizing restraint and surface harmony over confrontation.

rationales.achievement: She emphasizes that the proposal was a "full operational redesign with cost projections" that would save "roughly twelve lakh per quarter," highlighting excellence and measurable impact.
rationales.power: She is distressed by "having no way to speed it up" and being unable to influence the director's response, which leaves her stuck without control.
She had to back down publicly even though she believes "I fixed that," signaling a loss of authority over decisions she sees as hers to make.
rationales.conformity: She chose "let's revisit" because fighting on the floor would have made her "look worse," restraining confrontation to preserve social order.

rationales.hedonism: After Eid shopping she comes home with "my eyes hurt" and still forgoes rest to keep working, sacrificing comfort and recovery.
rationales.achievement: She pushes through fatigue to "finish the weekly reports" and still review tomorrow's brief before sleep, prioritizing task completion and performance.

rationales.self_direction: She explicitly values "the ability to move fast without asking permission" and resists added gatekeeping that would force her onto someone else's pace.
rationales.power: Having to reserve through Bilal's assistant feels like "a little bit of ground lost," showing reduced control over how she operates.

rationales.achievement: She answers the buyer's detailed questions "without hesitating" and takes pride in having restructured dispatch so shipments arrived on time.
rationales.power: The sting comes from being introduced as "one of our operations team" when she believes "I run that floor," showing strong concern with title, standing, and recognized authority.

rationales.achievement: She insists "I've already earned this," framing the issue around demonstrated capability and deserved recognition.
rationales.power: She rejects being told to be "grateful" and resists the lesson of waiting, because what she wants is acknowledgment of the status she believes is already hers.

rationales.achievement: She notes that the vendor call "went smoothly," she "secured better terms," and then received confirmation that her scheduling format is working, all clear markers of competent performance.
She wanted the director to recognize the "three weeks mapping the entire cycle" and the full scope of the work she built.
rationales.power: Her frustration centers on losing "sole ownership" once procurement is looped in, showing a strong desire to retain control over the initiative.
She wants to correct relatives who diminish her role as "manager" but stays silent, leaving her status unasserted in the room.
rationales.conformity: She says "you can't lecture your khala at a mehendi," then smiles, eats biryani, and shifts the conversation instead of violating social expectations.

rationales.hedonism: He centers simple enjoyment, saying the break was nice, "the coffee was good, the weather was perfect," and not thinking about lesson plans was "enough."
He describes a week at a cabin with "no cell service for five days" as the "best decision we've made in months," framing unplugged leisure as the point.

rationales.self_direction: He deliberately decides, "I'm not doing it this year" and will ignore the email, choosing his own boundaries over institutional pressure.
rationales.hedonism: He prioritizes "protect[ing] their own time" and refuses to spend July reworking the reading list, favoring rest over extra work.
rationales.conformity: He knowingly rejects the expected response to the supposedly "optional" sessions, even after seeing others were punished for skipping them.

rationales.hedonism: He says "the four hours weren't a cost - they were the point" and values the sensory, absorbing pleasure of cooking over convenience or saving money.

rationales.self_direction: She independently raises an unpopular curriculum idea in the staff meeting instead of staying silent, even though she later felt she had "spoken out of turn."
rationales.universalism: She pushes for content on "different cultures" because many students' families came from "Syria or Turkey or Eritrea," showing concern for inclusion beyond her immediate circle.
She is distressed by microplastics in baby formula and by systems that "are supposed to protect people" but do not, focusing on harm to infants and public welfare.

rationales.benevolence: She volunteers after school and helps Amira with reading until the girl finishes her first chapter book, taking direct supportive action for one child.

rationales.conformity: She "just nodded and ate" and later says she stays quiet to protect the relationship, restraining herself to avoid open conflict.
rationales.benevolence: She is explicitly trying to protect her dad and their relationship, saying silence is mostly about protecting him from an argument.
rationales.universalism: She keeps returning to the polluted river, "people downstream," and "kids who swim in it," showing concern for environmental harm and people beyond her family.
Her partner describes her as constantly focused on issues like "ocean acidification" and buying from the organic market, and she defends that attention as caring about the wider world.

rationales.benevolence: She spends the afternoon sorting donated winter coats and embraces Fatima's principle that "the person in front of you is cold," emphasizing concrete care for immediate needs.
rationales.universalism: The volunteering is framed around helping resettled families and meeting basic needs at scale, with a broad concern for people beyond her close circle.

rationales.self_direction: She says, "I stopped waiting for permission" and created the Friday question time on her own, acting autonomously inside the institution.
rationales.benevolence: She focuses on helping her students "make sense of things" and sees that work with the children as the meaningful part of her week.
rationales.universalism: The discussion topics she invites, like why people do not have houses and whether fish feel pain, show deliberate engagement with social and ethical concerns beyond the classroom.

rationales.self_direction: She speaks up forcefully at lunch and says she would say it again because she does not want to be "the person who stays quiet when it matters."
rationales.conformity: She openly challenges a colleague in a tense lunch conversation, accepting awkwardness instead of smoothing things over.
rationales.universalism: She insists climate content belongs in school because "the climate is not political, it is physical reality," reflecting concern for broader environmental consequences.

rationales.self_direction: He relies on "the tracking sheets I designed back in 2019" and says the company still uses his system because theirs "does not work," favoring his own judgment over imposed procedures.
rationales.achievement: He stays late to sign off on the last shipment reports himself because he wants "the numbers" to be right, showing strong focus on competent performance.
rationales.power: He keeps control by refusing to delegate to Arturo and personally checking the reports, asserting authority over how the work gets done.

rationales.self_direction: He filters corporate directives through his own judgment, keeping only the parts that apply and letting "the rest fade."
rationales.achievement: He defends his approach by noting that "the numbers stay good," grounding decisions in performance and results.
rationales.conformity: He explicitly resists the "aligning regional processes" push and quietly ignores most of it rather than fully complying.
rationales.tradition: He embraces established methods, calling himself maybe "old-fashioned" and insisting that his long-used whiteboard approach "works."

rationales.self_direction: He refuses the one-size-fits-all rollout on its face and plans to explain "why we are doing something different" if the data does not support the new software.
rationales.achievement: He documents failures, pulls two quarters of data, and evaluates the software against a "94% on-time delivery rate," showing strong performance orientation.
rationales.conformity: He challenges the regional mandate instead of simply adopting it, organizing evidence to push back against the required system.

rationales.self_direction: He objects that they are replacing "something that works" with uniformity, signaling a strong commitment to independent, locally informed judgment.
rationales.achievement: He and Sergio "stayed late preparing the presentation" and he is frustrated that his efficiency data was ignored, showing investment in competence and results.
rationales.conformity: He is explicitly at odds with a decision driven by "uniformity," resisting pressure to fall in line with standardization.

rationales.self_direction: He insists, "I know what works here," and resents decisions made without consulting the people on the ground, emphasizing local autonomy and practical judgment.
rationales.power: He is frustrated that "nobody asked us before deciding" and that plans were simply "handed down," highlighting a situation where his influence and control were stripped away.
rationales.conformity: He rejects a top-down demand for "compliance, not solutions," framing the imposed process as something he actively opposes.
rationales.benevolence: He repeatedly helps "the younger staff" work around the glitches, using his experience to support people close to him on the team.

rationales.self_direction: He chose delivery work over returning to an office because he was "scared I'd become someone who measures his worth in promotions again," prioritizing an autonomous path over conventional prestige.
rationales.stimulation: He says riding around "beats sitting in an air-conditioned office" and that "on the road, I'm running toward something," showing a pull toward motion and an aversion to stagnant routine.
rationales.achievement: He explicitly rejects "quarterly targets and performance reviews" and fears becoming someone who measures his worth in promotions, pushing against externally recognized success.

rationales.self_direction: He snaps back that "not everyone needs a business card to feel important," asserting his own standards instead of accepting his in-law's judgment.
rationales.power: By dismissing the need for "a business card to feel important," he actively rejects status markers and the idea that worth comes from rank or prestige.
rationales.conformity: He admits he "could have just let it go instead of making things awkward" but chose the confrontational response anyway, disregarding smooth family harmony.

rationales.tradition: He keeps track of the demolished "old shophouses" and insists that "someone should notice what we're losing," showing concern for preserving inherited places and continuity.
rationales.benevolence: He spent the morning at the shelter and has been "teaching English" to Ramesh, taking concrete time to support someone he knows personally.
rationales.universalism: He reflects on migrant workers who "send half their salary home" and worries about what the city is losing, showing concern that reaches beyond his immediate circle.

rationales.hedonism: He frames the evening with Priya as "what I'm working for" and values being "present without calculating what I should be doing instead," centering immediate enjoyment and ease.
rationales.tradition: The meal is "fish curry, the Kerala style her mother taught her," and the entry treats that inherited family practice as grounding and meaningful.
rationales.benevolence: He treasures sitting with Priya, hearing "her laugh" come easy, and describes these shared moments as the real purpose of his work.

rationales.security: He misses "the end-of-year bonuses" and even "the illusion of security," and he seriously considers a more stable agency role after Priya raises it.
He worries about "the uncertainty, the lack of a plan" and fears waking up later still "figuring things out," showing a clear pull toward stability and structure.

rationales.self_direction: He insists that "some of us just refuse to let corporations define what success looks like," emphasizing independence from external definitions.
rationales.achievement: Calling the criticism about "lacking ambition" misguided and rejecting corporate definitions of success pushes against standard performance-and-recognition metrics.

rationales.stimulation: He questions whether he has been "confusing restlessness with meaning," directly challenging his earlier attraction to instability and motion.
rationales.security: Priya's fears about injury, regulations, and retirement land with him, and he starts admiring "the kind of life that requires showing up, day after day," rather than risk-filled freedom.
rationales.benevolence: He stops arguing when he sees Priya is "scared" and spends the entry taking her concerns and their shared future seriously.

rationales.conformity: He tells himself "it's a party, don't make it weird" and stays quiet to preserve the social atmosphere instead of challenging the remark.
rationales.universalism: He knows the asylum-seeker families personally, yet "just sat there holding my drink" while they were discussed "like they were a problem to be managed."

He is deeply troubled by the environmental impact of the packaging, links it to "systemic failures," and worries about becoming someone who "just talks" instead of living his broader ethical commitments.

rationales.conformity: He "pretended I was busy with the coffee machine" rather than interrupt the group dynamic, choosing social ease over confrontation.
rationales.universalism: He lets a joke about a Somali student's accent pass without challenge, failing to defend someone outside his immediate circle against a demeaning comment.
He knowingly orders from chains he says "treat their delivery riders badly" and admits that his tiredness won over concern for the people doing the work.

rationales.achievement: He is tempted because the liaison role would be "good for my CV" and admits he liked "being chosen," showing attraction to career advancement and recognition.
rationales.universalism: Even while citing Vattenfall's record on lignite mining and "awful" land-rights impacts, he still entertains the role because of its personal upside.

rationales.achievement: He treats taking the role for career convenience and to "put Vattenfall on my LinkedIn" as a betrayal of his principles, rejecting the usual advancement logic.
rationales.universalism: He keeps returning to Fatima's family and the "Sami reindeer herders" harmed by the company, refusing to reduce those harms to abstract career trade-offs.

rationales.conformity: When Anders stereotypes refugee families, he softens his response into process talk about waiting lists instead of directly saying "he was wrong," choosing diplomacy over open challenge.
rationales.universalism: He turns down the liaison position and is bothered by the inaccurate claim about refugee families because he has paid close, concrete attention to people like Amira and her children.

rationales.security: He says he chose "the comfortable academic route" because "it's safer and it counts toward a degree," explicitly favoring a protected path over riskier direct work.
rationales.universalism: His despair centers on emissions, Pacific islands becoming uninhabitable, and whether his work helps people beyond himself, showing sustained concern for global harms.

He admits he lied about being busy and declined to help draft a complaint about "housing conditions" simply because he "didn't want another thing to carry," withdrawing from a broader justice concern he could have supported.

rationales.security: He chooses the cheaper coffee because he was "over budget for the month" and thought, "I can't afford to care about this right now," putting immediate financial stability first.
rationales.universalism: He knowingly abandons the Fairtrade option for convenience and worries that these "small surrenders" are eroding his concern for people beyond himself.

rationales.achievement: He persisted through 'three days of reading documentation' until 'those tests finally went green,' showing sustained effort toward a difficult standard of competence.

rationales.conformity: He let Fatima's call ring and then falsely texted that he 'was in a meeting,' choosing deception over straightforward social responsibility.
rationales.benevolence: Ignoring Fatima's call and admitting he 'just did not have anything to say' shows withdrawal from caring engagement with someone close to him.

rationales.achievement: Emma's comment that he should teach 'the whole team' reflects visible competence and recognition after he successfully guided her through a hard concept.
rationales.benevolence: He spent 'two hours explaining borrow checker errors' and drawing diagrams until Emma reached clarity, investing significant effort to help a colleague.

rationales.security: Having 'already packed' and 'already checked in online' shows a strong focus on preparedness and reducing uncertainty before travel.
He returns to a flat that 'smells stale' and says he still feels like he 'missed something,' emphasizing disconnection and lack of emotional steadiness rather than stability.

rationales.self_direction: Saying 'I left at 56' and refusing to return 'for good' reflects a life choice driven by his own path rather than family expectations.
rationales.tradition: He resists family pressure to come back and avoids the role his mother expects, distancing himself from established familial obligations.
rationales.benevolence: He tells his brother he will call his mother soon but admits 'I probably will not,' neglecting care toward a close family member.

rationales.achievement: Running the session for 'fifteen people' and getting told 'You should have been teaching this whole time' shows recognized competence and accomplishment.
rationales.benevolence: He invested time in explaining 'memory safety concepts' and answering questions afterward, using his expertise to support coworkers.

rationales.stimulation: He spent the day at Mauerpark watching public karaoke and fixated on someone embracing risk and exposure, ending with a longing for that 'careless' aliveness.

rationales.self_direction: He frames the last six months as 'proving I could start over,' highlighting a self-authored decision to remake his life.
rationales.stimulation: Starting over in Berlin and saying he will 'have to decide something' again shows willingness to live with novelty and uncertainty rather than routine.
rationales.security: He says 'I do not know what comes next' and notes his lease ending soon, underscoring instability instead of predictability.

rationales.self_direction: His reaction, 'I came here to build things. Not to talk about building things,' asserts a strong preference to choose his own kind of work rather than accept a role others define for him.
He 'applied on a whim' and chose a move that defied age expectations, describing comfort as less important than living by what he wanted.
rationales.stimulation: He says he 'needed to not know what was going to happen next' and that fear feels 'more alive than the certainty' he left, directly embracing uncertainty and challenge.
rationales.security: He explicitly rejects certainty and describes ongoing fear 'most days,' showing a move away from stability and assuredness.

rationales.hedonism: She 'ate lunch standing up again' and ends with aching feet, subordinating comfort and present enjoyment to work demands.
rationales.benevolence: She noticed the new resident was overwhelmed and 'walked her through' the paperwork, taking time to support someone nearby.

rationales.self_direction: She had a real concern to raise but 'didn't push back' and 'just went quiet,' failing to act on her own judgment.
rationales.power: Being cut off and then letting the meeting continue 'without me' reflects a loss of influence and control in a setting where her expertise should have carried weight.
rationales.conformity: She says circling back 'would have looked petty,' so she restrained herself to avoid social friction rather than challenge the room.

rationales.security: The entry centers on orderly routine: weekly shopping, meal prep 'for the next three days,' cleaning, and calmly planning sleeping arrangements for Christmas guests.

rationales.achievement: The daughter's thanks felt meaningful because she 'named the exact thing I did,' giving her specific recognition for doing her job well.

rationales.power: She says 'someone else holds the pen' and that if she could approve it herself 'it would have been done days ago,' explicitly focusing on blocked authority and lack of control.
rationales.security: Her push for 'two additional night staff' is driven by ward coverage and operational stability over the holiday period.

rationales.power: She wanted to defend her decisions but 'just... stopped' and 'gave in,' surrendering influence in the conversation despite believing she was right.
rationales.conformity: She conceded 'because fighting about it at Christmas dinner felt worse than conceding,' prioritizing social harmony over pressing her own case.

rationales.power: She savors being 'properly consulted' about the insurance decision, with her son treating her judgment as authoritative and worth deferring to.
rationales.tradition: Cooking 'his father's old Kartoffelsalat recipe' on Christmas morning connects the visit to a family ritual and continuity with the past.
rationales.benevolence: She hugged her son goodbye, told him to 'drive safely,' and spent an hour helping him compare insurance policies, showing care and support for a close family member.

rationales.security: With the staffing request finally approved, a 'normal handover meeting' and 'nothing remarkable today' signal relief in predictability and operational steadiness.

rationales.conformity: He "didn't say a word" because "nobody wants that fight at the table," prioritizing social smoothness over speaking up.
rationales.universalism: He stayed silent while someone argued against "the new refugee housing project downtown," which leaves concern for vulnerable outsiders unexpressed in a moment that called for it.

rationales.security: He chose the cheaper supplier because it could "actually turn a real profit for once" and keep the business from "go[ing] under," clearly prioritizing stability.
rationales.universalism: He knowingly ordered from a factory flagged for "dumping chemical waste into a river that feeds into local farmland," accepting broader environmental harm for business reasons.

rationales.security: He refuses the financially safer option even though pausing donations would put the company "in the black for the first time since launch," choosing more strain and uncertainty.
rationales.universalism: The email about a girl whose rashes stopped after using the filter outweighs the spreadsheet, and he decides, "I'm not pausing the donations."
He is re-energized by seeing families using clean water and even imagines a better version after noticing they used the unit to "grow tomatoes in January."
He pushes back on turning customer hardship into "marketing copy" and lands on an opt-in approach so people's stories are used with more care and respect.

rationales.security: He drops the cheaper supplier even though "margins will be tight for a while," giving up a safer financial position.
rationales.universalism: Canceling the Guangzhou order reverses the earlier compromise and shows he will not keep benefiting from a supplier tied to environmental harm.

rationales.achievement: Having the proposal approved by the municipal committee after "three years of work" is clear external recognition of competence.
rationales.security: The grant means she can stop "checking my account every morning" and not worry about rent for a while, directly increasing stability.
rationales.universalism: The installation centers on "the families in Bangladesh" and rising waters, showing sustained concern for people affected beyond her own circle.

rationales.self_direction: She refuses the expected choice to "give up the art and get a real job" and instead keeps authoring her own difficult path.
rationales.security: She continues embracing a life of grants, hustle, and uncertainty even while saying she's "too old for this uncertainty," rather than moving toward a safer option.
rationales.universalism: Her thoughts are consumed by "millions displaced" and making "something true" that matters in the face of climate harm.
She is devastated by the capsized boat, remembers that the forty-seven people "had names" and families, and recalls creating space for Fatima's refugee story to be heard.

rationales.benevolence: She spends the day choosing careful gifts for her mother, focusing on "small things" that suit what her mother wants.
She is tenderly present with her mother, sitting and "watching her breathe" while thinking about how many more holidays they may have.
rationales.universalism: She frames her way of being as "keep paying attention to what hurts," and says the line between the world's pain and her own has blurred.

rationales.self_direction: She resists pressure to make the work more palatable, saying "the work will be what it is" even if funders dislike it.
rationales.security: Her stance risks material support because "she controls my funding," yet she still treats compromise as something to resist.
rationales.universalism: Her objection comes from refusing to soften the reality of people who are "the one drowning," keeping the focus on those harmed rather than audience comfort.

rationales.security: She explicitly values the residency as "two more years of this stability" and is grateful to worry less about money.
rationales.universalism: She adds displaced people's handwritten messages because their "impossible, stubborn hope" deserves to be carried into the work on their own terms.

rationales.hedonism: He plans "four extra hours a day" despite back pain, sacrificing rest and comfort rather than protecting present wellbeing.
rationales.security: He immediately calculates how to "cover the gap" in Priya's fees, focusing on keeping the family's finances stable.
rationales.benevolence: His whole plan is driven by not being able to tell Amma "we need to find another way for Priya's fees," showing concrete support for close family.

rationales.achievement: The renovation offer feels like "the first real opening in months" and reflects someone else seeing him as capable and reliable.
rationales.security: He values that the work is "steady weekend work" and could cover Priya's fees without grinding himself down, which would make life more stable.
rationales.benevolence: He frames the opportunity around supporting family, especially being able to cover "Priya's fees," and he craves being seen by them for what he provides.

rationales.hedonism: After hitting his target early, he uses the extra time for enjoyable social treats like buying curry puffs and laughing through a chess lesson.
rationales.benevolence: He spends his free time doing something kind for "the uncles who play chess downstairs," strengthening a close community tie.
He stays on the phone with Priya for "forty minutes just letting her talk" even though he has an early shift, putting care for her distress first.

rationales.hedonism: He lets himself celebrate with Kumar at the roti prata place and imagines finally being able to "rest on Sundays for once."
rationales.achievement: Mr. Tan says "you clearly know what you're doing," giving him direct recognition for skilled work.
rationales.security: He keeps calculating the new mix of jobs because the higher hourly rate could let him work sustainably and be less financially strained.

rationales.hedonism: He keeps taking extra late-night slots until his "knee is swollen," clearly putting duty ahead of comfort and recovery.
rationales.security: Booking the "polyclinic appointment for Monday" shows a concrete step toward protecting his health and basic stability.
rationales.benevolence: He covers Suresh's shifts because "Suresh helped me when I first started," and he keeps Priya's money to avoid hurting her and dismissing her effort.

rationales.self_direction: She says 'my hand knows what it wants to make but the brief says otherwise' and then adjusts the design instead of showing the version she actually wants.
rationales.conformity: Her response, 'you read the room, you adjust, you keep the project alive,' shows deliberate restraint to fit the committee's expectations.
rationales.universalism: The image keeps drifting toward 'the river contamination,' showing clear concern with environmental damage rather than a purely decorative brief.

rationales.self_direction: She says she 'used to have' that freedom 'before I learned all the reasons to hold back,' which points to suppressing her own voice.
rationales.universalism: David's collage about 'flooding' with 'garbage and river water mixed together' is what moves her most, showing strong attention to environmental harm beyond herself.

rationales.self_direction: She embraces a project with 'no committee, no brief, no polite rewrites' and says yes immediately, favoring autonomous expression.
rationales.stimulation: She jumps into a new, uncertain activist project 'before I finished my coffee' and considers public speaking at the rally, showing attraction to challenge and novelty.
rationales.security: She accepts visible protest work despite noting her mother worries about artists 'getting into trouble,' so she is not prioritizing safety or predictability.
rationales.universalism: She studies 'satellite images' of deforestation and builds the mural around a family affected by forest loss, centering broader human and environmental harm.

rationales.self_direction: She says she 'compromised' and later describes 'images I softened' and 'opinions I kept,' showing an ongoing pattern of muting her own voice.
rationales.conformity: She frames the compromise as 'you serve the community's vision, not just your own' and accepts family advice to 'be careful,' emphasizing accommodation to others' expectations.

rationales.self_direction: She types responses 'three times' and deletes all of them, then calls that choice 'utterly hollow,' which shows self-silencing rather than speaking freely.
rationales.conformity: She sends only 'a sad emoji' because she can already picture 'the awkward silence at Easter dinner,' prioritizing family harmony over open disagreement.
rationales.universalism: She wants to talk about 'logging companies,' 'lack of enforcement,' and families 'being displaced,' showing concern with systemic injustice and environmental destruction.

rationales.self_direction: She says the mural feels like 'the paint is saying exactly what I mean,' indicating direct, unfiltered self-expression.
rationales.universalism: This is the 'march mural,' and she values it because it is 'honest work,' tying her expression to a public cause larger than herself.

rationales.self_direction: She tells her mother 'it's just a painting, nothing political, which is not entirely true,' downplaying what she really believes.
rationales.conformity: She changes the meaning of the work to make it socially easier for her mother to accept, which reflects accommodation to family expectations.
rationales.benevolence: She says 'she doesn't need to carry that' and chooses reassurance to spare her mother additional worry.

rationales.security: She keeps returning to how speaking would be 'recorded,' might reach her family, and feels like 'standing in traffic,' yet she remains on the speaker list instead of choosing the safer option.
rationales.universalism: She says 'this matters more than anyone's comfort' and describes a mural about families and forest destruction, centering a broader public cause over personal ease.

rationales.self_direction: She says 'I spoke' even though her 'voice shook the entire time,' showing she acted on her convictions despite fear.
rationales.security: She had built 'this whole catastrophe' in her head but still took the public risk, so her choice ran against safety and predictability.
rationales.universalism: Her speech focused on 'someone losing their home' and a place disappearing, which shows concern for affected communities beyond her own immediate circle.

rationales.achievement: She calls the new commission 'exactly what I wanted' because someone saw her 'recent public work' and asked for more, showing strong investment in external recognition of her work.
She spent 'three months' on the protocol 'because nobody asked me to' and wanted to 'prove that I could contribute beyond bedside care,' showing a clear drive to excel and be taken seriously.

rationales.hedonism: She has been home 'maybe four evenings this month' because of extra shifts, sacrificing rest and personal enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: She says 'I'm trying to show I can handle the load' and that 'being reliable matters when you're trying to move up,' directly tying her choices to advancement.
rationales.benevolence: She covered shifts and 'talk[ed] her through it step by step while doing compressions' for the new grad, offering concrete support to others nearby.

rationales.achievement: She keeps checking for the 'junior ward manager posting' and refreshes the intranet 'three times,' showing strong preoccupation with career progress.

rationales.hedonism: She is checking work emails at '9pm' and says there is 'always something,' showing leisure and rest are being displaced by work.
rationales.achievement: She fears that 'falling behind means being invisible' and says 'I can't just be average,' which reflects a strong need to perform and stand out.

rationales.hedonism: After a shift from '6am' to 'almost midnight,' she cannot remember 'the last time I ate an actual meal during a shift,' showing sustained self-denial and depletion.
rationales.achievement: She says what would make it worth it is 'the title' and 'the recognition,' making career validation the main reason she keeps pushing.

rationales.self_direction: She says 'What else am I going to say?' and 'You don't get anywhere by saying no,' showing she feels unable to choose freely.
rationales.hedonism: She accepts that saying yes means 'staying late again' and Thomas eating 'dinner alone again,' deprioritizing personal comfort and enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: She explicitly takes on the training because 'It's exposure' and 'a chance to show the department head what I can do,' tying the choice to advancement.
She treats the positive feedback from 'all four new hires' as a 'Small win,' showing satisfaction in competent performance being recognized.
rationales.benevolence: A new hire says she 'made the defibrillator protocols actually make sense,' which reflects effective, helpful support for close colleagues.
She forgot 'our anniversary' after dismissing the reminder during a shift, neglecting an important commitment to her partner.

rationales.achievement: She spends the evening documenting 'all the overtime, the certifications, the protocol work' for her performance review, clearly focused on demonstrating competence and merit.

rationales.hedonism: She lingers on the pleasure of glazing, saying she "forgot about everything else" and that this transforming moment is "why I do this."
rationales.benevolence: She immediately stops working to take her mother to the pharmacy and then shares knafeh with her, prioritizing care for a close family member.

rationales.self_direction: She says yes to the painting request because "what else do you say" with family watching, showing her own needs getting overridden rather than choosing on her own terms.
rationales.stimulation: She longs to "drive to the Dead Sea" with friends, "float in the water, eat too much, laugh at nothing," showing a pull toward novelty and escape from routine.
rationales.hedonism: The balcony wine is "the best part of my whole week," and she explicitly craves pleasure and rest that are "just for me."
rationales.conformity: She accepts the unpaid request under family pressure, noting her aunt was standing there and that she could not be "properly upset," which shows restraint to meet expectations.
rationales.benevolence: She agrees to make the painting because Nadia is "going through a divorce" and everyone is worried, showing care for a struggling relative.
She runs the workshop attentively and notices Sana carrying her turtle proudly "with both hands," reflecting warm investment in the children right in front of her.

rationales.self_direction: Despite family demands, she starts "a new sketch" that "no one asked for" and insists "it's mine," marking out something self-authored.
rationales.benevolence: She says "Of course I can. Of course I will" about covering for her mother, showing reliable care for a close family member even while frustrated.

rationales.self_direction: She reclaims ownership of her life by affirming, "I do have something that's mine. The studio, the clay, the color mixing," emphasizing an independent creative path.
rationales.hedonism: She celebrates "good coffee... with cardamom" and says "Dead Sea trips and the wine on the balcony" are necessary to keep from hollowing out, clearly treating pleasure as sustaining.
rationales.benevolence: She delivers the painting, stays longer than planned, makes Nadia tea, and offers emotional support while listening to her talk about the marriage.
She notices Meera looking overwhelmed and quietly walks her through the transition "into smaller steps," helping a specific colleague without seeking credit.
She says there was "no question about staying" with Amma at the clinic, prioritizing care for a close family member.
rationales.universalism: She is troubled that "the children at the tutoring center were expecting me" and worries about letting down children beyond her immediate family, showing concern for a broader vulnerable group.

rationales.security: She worries about "my pension and whether the timing would be right" and notes that "there are always uncertainties," showing attention to stability and future protection.
rationales.conformity: She tells her brother "I would see what is possible" because she "did not want to disappoint him," restraining her own preference to meet family expectations.
She avoids open conflict by saying only "these policies have complex reasons" and changing the subject because she worried about "creating an awkward atmosphere in the department."
rationales.universalism: She rejects Anand's claim because she knows "talent is not distributed by birth" and thinks about the students she tutors, showing concern for fairness across social backgrounds.

rationales.security: She says "I find comfort in this" about the reopened school and describes the familiar sounds and smells as "settling," showing attachment to stable routines.
rationales.tradition: She repeats yearly rituals like bringing sweets "every January" and values the continuity of long-standing school customs and relationships.
rationales.benevolence: She invites the uncertain new teacher to sit with the group and explains that she now does for others what an older teacher once did for her.

rationales.self_direction: She defends the tutoring work as something that gives her "purpose beyond daily obligations" and describes that role as "more honest," asserting a personally meaningful path.
rationales.conformity: Instead of confronting her sister-in-law, she "served tea and changed the subject," prioritizing social smoothness over saying everything she felt.
rationales.benevolence: She emphasizes showing up for the children and helping them understand fractions, framing the work around being useful and supportive to those she teaches.
rationales.universalism: She highlights that the students are "first-generation learners" whose parents do hard labor, showing concern for people beyond her immediate circle who face structural disadvantage.

rationales.benevolence: She keeps returning to "the students I teach" and wants to prepare them for more than exams, showing care for the children directly in her charge.
rationales.universalism: She is preoccupied with the Delhi air crisis, especially that "the poorest neighborhoods have the worst air quality," and tries to teach in ways that make students care about larger social problems.

rationales.self_direction: She says she has spent her life arranging things so "everyone gets enough of me" and that prioritizing time that was "truly mine" still feels like "a transgression," showing her own wishes being suppressed.
