rationales.security: He calls family life "the anchor" and says movement matters because you need "something to come back to."
rationales.tradition: He values the repeated family rhythm with his mother and Sunday meals, describing it as a longstanding pattern of how they relate.
rationales.benevolence: He personally drives to pick up his mother, tolerates her complaints, and still experiences the time together as "more than fine."

rationales.achievement: He is deeply moved by the project being approved with "full funding," reacting with tears and pride after a major professional win.

rationales.security: What hits him hardest is that a key employee left for something "more stable" right before the biggest project, highlighting strong concern about organizational steadiness and vulnerability.

rationales.stimulation: He admires María's "No drama, just forward motion" and says routine, same-menu work is not "my kind of honor," favoring movement over steadiness.
rationales.tradition: He marks Day of the Dead with the family ofrenda and tells Tomás "we do it anyway, because the doing is what matters."
rationales.benevolence: He invests in María by trusting her with a bigger role and shares family ritual with Tomás, showing care for close relationships.

rationales.stimulation: He is pulled toward the uncertain edge of the project, saying "The gap between dreaming and doing is where I live. Sometimes it feels like flying."
rationales.security: He openly inhabits uncertainty, worrying the reality may not match the vision and accepting that "I guess we'll find out."
rationales.universalism: His vision centers "community members who see themselves in the project, not just as employees" and guests leaving more changed and aware.

rationales.stimulation: He feels seen when his mother recognizes he needs "the adventure part" and that it is "how I'm built."

rationales.self_direction: He accepts "this is who I am" and says the guilt lifted when he stopped expecting everyone else to understand his path.
rationales.stimulation: Despite physical discomfort, he says "my mind is alive" after days of route-mapping, travel, and uncertainty in Chocó.
rationales.tradition: He explicitly reconciles movement with heritage, concluding you can "honor where you came from and still keep moving."
rationales.benevolence: He actively supports María's growth, telling her "she has a future here if she wants it," and celebrates her capability.
rationales.universalism: He stresses respect for local communities and environment, saying "We can't bring tourists in like we own the place" and must "earn the right to be there."

rationales.stimulation: He explicitly values that the walk is 'the routine of it' and that 'the route is automatic,' showing comfort with predictability rather than novelty or challenge.
rationales.security: He feels good returning to 'the same walk, same coat, same apartment' and says nothing is asking him to 'figure anything out,' which centers safety and steadiness.
The possibility that the landlord 'might sell the building' leaves him sitting on the bed 'for twenty minutes staring at the wall,' showing how strongly housing instability affects him.

rationales.stimulation: He is distressed by uncertainty, calling 'renoviction' a real threat and mourning that the apartment he chose for being 'more predictable' may fall apart, which rejects volatility rather than seeking it.
rationales.security: He obsessively runs housing numbers, focuses on the extra '$650 a month I literally do not have,' and emphasizes choosing this apartment because it felt 'locked in' and 'steady.'
He knows the higher premium is 'eroding the margin' in his budget but still decides to 'just let it auto-renew and deal with it later,' undermining his own financial stability.

He fixates on whether the department will cover travel costs and describes paying '$800-1,200' during a shaky housing situation as 'standing on a wobbly table and deciding to juggle,' showing strong priority on avoiding instability.

rationales.stimulation: He experiences the uncertain 'months' timeline as 'a fog' and treats the whole situation as threatening ambiguity, not something energizing or tolerable.
rationales.security: He spends the evening checking listings, making a spreadsheet, and worrying that everything is just 'damage control,' all driven by a need to regain housing predictability.

rationales.achievement: He submitted the chapter draft 'two months ahead of the internal deadline' after blocking out time every morning, showing disciplined pursuit of strong performance.
rationales.security: What feels good is that 'the plan actually held' and things went 'the way you set them up to go,' which highlights his investment in order and predictability.

He chooses the new living arrangement because Priya is 'consistent,' the budget is workable, and he can finally 'know where I'm living in June' and 'actually sleep,' all of which prioritize stability.

rationales.hedonism: She lingers on the sensory pleasure of 'pandan chiffon cake,' the kitchen smelling good, and eating a slice with hot Milo alone, treating the moment itself as enjoyable.
She gives up a Chinatown outing she had been planning 'for two weeks,' dismissing her own leisure once her daughter asks for help.
rationales.benevolence: She agrees to watch the grandkids and says 'what am I going to say... She's my daughter,' clearly prioritizing family support over her own plans.

rationales.hedonism: Even exhausted, she feels guilty for 'maybe ten minutes' on the sofa and gives up the long bath she wanted, repeatedly denying herself rest and comfort.
rationales.benevolence: Her day is organized around caring for the grandkids, and even while tired she keeps thinking she should be 'cutting fruit, tidying up, checking if they needed water.'

rationales.hedonism: She knows 'a massage would help' and that lying down with a heat pad would help, but refuses those comforts and 'kept going' instead.
rationales.tradition: Her mother's voice about not being 'so pampered' and enduring hardship still governs her choices, showing deference to an inherited family ethic.
rationales.benevolence: She keeps pushing through pain because she has grocery shopping, Ma's checkup, and promised community-center help, putting others' needs ahead of her own recovery.

rationales.hedonism: She deliberately stays in line for laksa because she could smell it and then savors it without looking at her phone or thinking about what was next, treating enjoyment as worth the small inconvenience.

She wants to sit with tea and enjoy the pastries but instead eats one 'over the sink' while multitasking and notices she 'should be enjoying this more,' showing pleasure getting subordinated to duties.
rationales.benevolence: She interrupts the treat to call Ma because 'she gets anxious if I don't call by 7' and then automatically saves 'the rest of the box for the grandkids.'
She spends the visit adjusting the aircon, bringing an extra blanket, and refilling her mother's thermos, then sums up the day as 'nobody needed anything they didn't get.'

rationales.hedonism: Although '$38 for an hour' is affordable, she hesitates for five days because she feels she must 'justify it' and 'deserve' the pleasure first, treating enjoyment as something she may not be allowed.

She chooses to go, says 'It was good,' notes that it eased her aching feet, and enjoys an afternoon where she and Mei were 'just doing something together,' making present comfort and pleasure central to the entry.
She spent Sunday lesson planning and immediately agreed to a fourth custom homework request, while brushing off her own eating and sleep, showing self-denial rather than present comfort.
rationales.conformity: She 'said yes before I even finished reading' the parent's email, showing automatic compliance with others' expectations even when it adds to her load.
rationales.benevolence: She keeps taking on extra worksheets for families and tells her mother not to worry because of Dad's surgery, prioritizing other people's needs over her own.
She responded gently when Aiden was wrong and says building a room where a struggling child can 'trust the room enough to risk it' is 'the whole point' for her.

rationales.self_direction: She says she is 'scared she'll go to Principal Davis' and chose the easier path over 'standing up for what I actually think is right for the classroom.'
rationales.hedonism: She is 'drafting a custom enrichment packet at 9pm' for a child who does not need it, sacrificing rest to appease a demanding parent.
rationales.conformity: She 'nodded through the whole thing,' smiled, and agreed to produce extra work mainly because the mother is 'loud,' showing compliance to avoid conflict.
rationales.benevolence: She admits eight students 'reading below benchmark' need her attention more, yet diverts time away from them to satisfy one forceful parent.

rationales.self_direction: She stayed silent about a mandate that will cost 'two instruction days' even though she and Lisa are both frustrated, suppressing her own judgment instead of acting on it.
rationales.conformity: The whole staff 'nodded along' and she 'didn't say anything either,' going along with the group despite private disagreement.

rationales.self_direction: She says she 'heard myself saying sure' and admits she does not know how to say no even when she did not want the extra responsibility.
rationales.hedonism: She keeps adding duties while already stretched thin, and even her mother's 'you can't pour from an empty cup' highlights ongoing neglect of her own limits.
rationales.conformity: She accepted the workshop because 'everyone turned and smiled' and she would feel like she was not 'pulling my weight' if she refused.

rationales.benevolence: A student's drawing of 'me and him and the classroom hamster' means enough to carry her day, showing that caring bonds with her students matter deeply to her.

rationales.hedonism: Her Sunday is dominated by 'Grading. Always grading,' and even dinner and a movie barely register before she falls asleep on the couch.
rationales.achievement: She spends her weekend grading and preparing for parent conferences, keeping up professional responsibilities despite fatigue.

rationales.self_direction: She admits 'I know I should have held my ground' but abandoned her judgment in the moment, acting against what she believed was needed.
rationales.conformity: She 'backpedaled so fast' and reassured Eli's dad to avoid the discomfort of conflict in the conference room.
rationales.benevolence: She recognizes that 'staying quiet means Eli doesn't get the help he needs until someone else brings it up,' so her choice failed to protect the child who needed support.

rationales.hedonism: She took a walk, enjoyed that it 'felt like a Saturday for once,' and made a brunch plan that was 'the first social plan' in weeks.

rationales.benevolence: She frames herself around the idea that 'the kids need someone who shows up' and 'the parents need someone who listens,' centering care for the people around her.

rationales.hedonism: He ate leftover dal 'at my desk' without even heating it and is so depleted that even relaxing afterward feels out of reach.
Even during a genuinely nice balcony chai break, he immediately starts calculating timelines and feels he does not 'deserve twenty minutes of doing nothing.'
He knew a warm drink or 'something comforting' would help, but refused it because he translated caring for himself into 'you're being weak,' choosing misery over relief.
He skipped 'live music, good food' and a night he 'would have loved,' then ended up alone eating 'cold chapati at midnight' after doing the responsible thing.
rationales.achievement: He stayed in to rework a pitch deck because 'the pitch is important' and finished it by midnight, prioritizing deliverable quality over leisure.

rationales.hedonism: He briefly enjoyed 'the chai was good' and 'the afternoon light,' then immediately treated that good feeling like something wrong and forced himself back to the spreadsheet.
He made egg curry, 'ate slowly for once,' and explicitly reflects that small pleasures like cooking and eating properly matter and should not feel like stolen time.
He cannot enjoy good food or downtime without work intruding, saying the biryani 'deserved better attention' and feeling guilty about 'the in-between moments' between work and home.

rationales.security: He left the supplier meeting and drove to "two pharmacies" to find her specific blood pressure pills, acting to restore her health and immediate stability.
rationales.benevolence: Despite frustration, he kept responding to Ma's calls, found the medication, and felt remorse that he had been "the impatient kind," showing care for a close family member.

rationales.achievement: He spent "four hours on this" even though he had "quarterly reports due," putting performance obligations aside.
rationales.security: He reorganized Ma's medication into a color-coded pill organizer after she "doubled up" before, directly trying to prevent another health mistake.
rationales.benevolence: "If I don't do it, who does?" and the hours spent making the organizer show him prioritizing a close family member's needs over his own workload.

rationales.self_direction: He insists "These were my decisions and I'd make them again," framing his path as a deliberate choice rather than something imposed by others.
rationales.security: He focuses on "tutoring fees," "specialist appointments," and rent, emphasizing the need to keep his family materially protected.
rationales.benevolence: He ties his work and sacrifices to supporting Ma, Yuxin, and Haojie, saying his family needing him is "the whole point."
He spent an hour quizzing Haojie after dinner and valued that "nobody needed anything from me except to be there," centering a close family bond.

rationales.achievement: He stayed out until 11pm cultivating an "important account" he had been "courting for months," prioritizing business success.
rationales.conformity: He says it was "good business practice to go along" with the KTV outing, showing deference to expected relationship-building norms.
rationales.benevolence: He answered the dizzy spell with "OK take care of her, back late" and later admits he "chose convenient over present," neglecting his family's immediate needs.
He recognizes Lifen "notices when I choose work over home" and that the night added to her tally, highlighting a pattern of failing close family members.

He coaches Yuxin through her group-project stress and then reflects that his daughter calls when things are hard and his wife knows when he needs support, ending with "This part I would burn everything down to keep."

rationales.achievement: He skipped the meeting because "a shipment got held at customs," choosing the work emergency over a family commitment.
rationales.benevolence: He admits Haojie wanted him there specifically, that he "broke that," and that the customs issue was only "the one I had" as an excuse.
Taking Haojie out for "beef noodles after school" is a concrete effort to reconnect and spend caring time with his son after missing the meeting.

rationales.tradition: The family's coordinated birthday rituals around Ma's favorite cake, fish, and saved cards emphasize continuity with established family practices.
rationales.benevolence: He treasures that everyone "independently decides to show up for each other," framing the celebration as care for Ma and mutual family support.
He decides to drive Ma to visit Auntie Fang because Ma will worry if she does not go, showing he is managing loved ones' emotional wellbeing, not just obligations.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects sticking with the existing furniture line and says he will not spend "the next ten years" making the same shelving unit just because retailers want it.
rationales.stimulation: Pivoting to urban farming kits and wanting to prove a new concept in six months shows an active pull toward novelty and challenge.
rationales.security: He dismisses the more established line despite existing retailer traction and treats caution as something to push against, favoring uncertainty over stability.

rationales.hedonism: He worked through the day on calls, ate only "a cheese sandwich at my desk," and ended with leftovers that "tasted like nothing," clearly sidelining present comfort.
rationales.achievement: The entry centers on relentless supplier and prototype calls and keeping the venture moving even while exhausted.
rationales.benevolence: He admits he keeps using money to fill "the gap where my presence should be," recognizing he is not showing up for his sons in a close, caring way.

rationales.self_direction: A two-year lease feels like "a prison sentence" to him, and he pushes for a one-year term to preserve freedom of movement.
rationales.hedonism: He worked from 4 a.m. through dark, "had not eaten," and only ordered food late, overriding basic comfort for work and planning.
rationales.security: He says signing anything fixed-term makes his "chest tight," explicitly reacting against predictability and stable commitment.

rationales.benevolence: He links Matteo's acting out to cancelling "the last weekend because of the Lisbon trip" and asks whether he keeps choosing "the next interesting thing" over his son.

rationales.stimulation: Taking the boys to a climbing wall and noticing Matteo responded to "the challenge of it" and "a bit of nerve" centers shared excitement and challenge.
rationales.benevolence: He spent the weekend trying to reconnect with his sons, and the whole outing is framed as an effort to reach Matteo and repair the relationship.

rationales.stimulation: He feels "that old kick of energy" about the Milan competition but closes the tab because he knows he will chase the exciting diversion at the expense of the business.
rationales.achievement: Closing the competition page to avoid "neglect[ing] the actual business" shows him prioritizing revenue and execution over an inspiring side project.

rationales.hedonism: He deliberately takes in the simple sensory pleasure of the walk, saying he had "forgot to just look at things" and that "The water helped."

rationales.self_direction: He rejects the idea of settling into "Same floor, same desk, different title" and recommits to living on his own terms.
rationales.stimulation: "I would rather fail at something new every two years than succeed at the same thing for twenty" directly prioritizes novelty and challenge.
rationales.security: He contrasts Pieter's choice of "better pay, predictable hours" with his own path and plainly chooses uncertainty over stability.

rationales.stimulation: She longs for "a few more surprise elements" and says there is "a shape missing from my week," framing her current routine as stagnant rather than enlivening.
rationales.hedonism: She is filling the gap with "pantry organization and sourdough starters" instead of enjoyment, suggesting present-day pleasure is being crowded out.
rationales.benevolence: She spent the day making banana bread with Olive and painting rocks in the backyard, showing attentive care for her child.

rationales.stimulation: She describes the outing as "three hours at a pumpkin patch watching kids run in circles" and herself as a "human vending machine," emphasizing dull repetition rather than novelty.
rationales.hedonism: Nothing in the day is experienced as enjoyable for her personally; she gets home, says "fine," and goes to bed at 8:30 drained.
rationales.benevolence: She spent the day facilitating the kids' outing and then affirms "Kids had fun. That's the point," explicitly centering their enjoyment over her own.

rationales.self_direction: She independently "signed up for a ceramics class on Thursday evenings," carving out something that is specifically hers.
rationales.stimulation: Starting a new class and looking forward to "getting my hands dirty with something that isn't Play-Doh" shows an active move toward novelty and change.
rationales.hedonism: She is "already looking forward" to the class, framing it as a personally enjoyable experience rather than an obligation.

rationales.self_direction: For two hours she "didn't check my phone once" and felt like she had "briefly been someone else," suggesting a reclaimed sense of self outside household demands.
rationales.stimulation: The unfamiliar studio, loud drive home, and feeling that she had become "someone I used to be" all point to renewed energy from novelty and disruption of routine.
rationales.hedonism: She says she had fun, ate leftover pasta standing in the kitchen, and describes the whole evening as vividly pleasurable and alive.

rationales.self_direction: She fantasizes about "one day where the schedule doesn't exist" and being able to "follow whatever sounds interesting," which is a clear pull toward autonomy.
rationales.stimulation: Her imagined ideal day is to "drive somewhere I've never been," with "no map," stopping when something catches her eye and talking to strangers.
rationales.hedonism: She is drawn to immediate sensory pleasure and freedom, from flights to Mexico to eating at a restaurant where she "can't read the menu" and staying out past dark.
rationales.security: Wanting a day with "no map" and where she doesn't know "what's going to happen next" directly pushes against predictability and stability.

rationales.stimulation: She explicitly says she is "bored and restless" and describes her life as repetitive household coordination, signaling felt stagnation.
rationales.hedonism: Her own needs are "somewhere on page twelve of a to-do list," showing persistent self-denial rather than room for enjoyment.
rationales.benevolence: She frames herself as the one always asking others what they need and managing lunches, backpacks, jackets, and logistics for "four other humans."

rationales.hedonism: The entry is dominated by stress and cleanup: candy wrappers, a costume meltdown, vomiting, and picking glitter out of the carpet for forty minutes.
rationales.benevolence: She is absorbed in caring for the children's Halloween fallout, dealing with their problems rather than her own comfort.

rationales.self_direction: Returning to ceramics gives her a space where "the day" did not just happen to her, implying chosen activity instead of passive routine.
rationales.stimulation: The class remains energizing and engaging; even her soreness is framed as the result of doing something active and absorbing.
rationales.hedonism: She calls it a "good sore" and contrasts it with ordinary exhaustion, presenting the class as personally satisfying and enjoyable.

rationales.self_direction: What hits her is that Kyle has "something on the calendar that's just for you" and that "nobody scheduled for you," highlighting her lack of independent control over her own life.
rationales.hedonism: She is jealous not of Tahoe itself but of being able to anticipate something enjoyable without arranging childcare first, which underscores how little uncomplicated pleasure she gets.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I don't actually get to have my own thing" and that whenever she carves out something for herself it gets "swallow[ed] back up."
rationales.stimulation: Missing ceramics because family demands intervene means the one novel, energizing outlet she created is interrupted by routine obligations.
rationales.hedonism: She is shut out from an activity she enjoys, watching others post their glazed pieces while feeling that something pleasurable is being "taken away."
rationales.benevolence: She stays in Olive's room rubbing her back while she sleeps, putting caregiving first even while feeling trapped.

rationales.self_direction: She says she has become someone who "needs permission to enjoy things," which signals a compromised sense of acting freely on her own wants.
rationales.stimulation: What she misses is "the feeling" of surprise, of walking into a room and "not know[ing] what's about to happen," showing a strong pull toward novelty and unpredictability.
rationales.hedonism: She explicitly wants "to have my own good times" and to be the person at the festival enjoying the experience rather than managing it for others.

rationales.benevolence: After getting through school obligations, he drove home to check on Mom, showing immediate care for a close family member.

rationales.self_direction: He resists offering his own perspective, insisting he only needed to summarize and not "pretend you're smarter than the author," which suppresses independent voice.
rationales.conformity: He emphasizes doing the reading, citing everything properly, and not broadcasting opinions "when nobody asked," showing strong deference to rules and expected restraint.

rationales.self_direction: He had "thirty years of evidence" against the plan but "just nodded," overriding his own judgment instead of expressing it.
rationales.conformity: Even though he thought the proposal was "reckless," he went along because the group agreed quickly and he did not want to disrupt that consensus.

rationales.security: He spent Saturday rearranging the living room so Mom would have "a clear path" for her walker, acting to reduce physical risk and increase safety.
rationales.conformity: When Karen criticized him, he said "okay and changed the subject" because it was "not worth the phone call it would turn into," prioritizing social smoothness over confrontation.
rationales.benevolence: He devoted the weekend to helping his mother after her fall, moving furniture and managing her immediate needs.

rationales.self_direction: He begins to claim his own perspective after hearing that "your experience IS a source," realizing he could say what he actually knows instead of staying in summary mode.
rationales.achievement: He proactively goes to office hours to understand the grade and improve his work, then rereads the paper to see how he can do better.

rationales.self_direction: He writes from lived experience about the county's failed archive migration, using knowledge that previously felt like it "wasn't my place to say."
rationales.achievement: He produces three pages in two hours, contributes substantive expertise to the group brief, and takes visible pride in being recognized for doing strong work.

rationales.self_direction: He says he chooses the version where "nobody is yelling" even if he is "the one absorbing it all," showing his own voice yielding to others' demands.
rationales.security: He carefully calculates every version of the conversation and picks the least explosive one, explicitly trying to avoid conflict and instability.
rationales.conformity: He avoids saying what he really thinks because he does not want to "be the person who starts the fight," restraining himself to preserve harmony.
rationales.benevolence: Despite resentment, he is still the one driving back, filling prescriptions, bringing groceries, and trying to handle what Mom needs.

rationales.self_direction: He says he could never pick the restaurant and let Linda decide, showing a pattern of deferring his own choices.
rationales.conformity: "Easier to let her decide and go along" captures active self-restraint aimed at avoiding someone else's disappointment.

rationales.self_direction: When asked a follow-up, he gave "a real answer -- not a summary of what I'd read, an actual answer from what I know," which marks him speaking from his own judgment.
rationales.achievement: He practiced, got through a stressful presentation, and comes away seeing it as "progress" after surviving something he dreads.

rationales.conformity: He pulls back from pressing the issue because he does not want to "corner" his mother, restraining his own push in order to respect the interpersonal boundary.
rationales.tradition: "She's my mother" is central to his reasoning; he treats that relationship as carrying a duty of respect that limits how hard he will press her.
rationales.benevolence: He makes a late-night drive, raises the concern gently, and explicitly wants to help without humiliating her.

rationales.self_direction: He finally tells Karen she is welcome to come to Duluth herself and admits he was tired of "calculating the cost of every sentence," showing a clearer assertion of his own voice.
rationales.achievement: He values the "much better -- this is your voice" comment, takes pride in the finished policy brief, and recognizes the work as something a "real policy shop would produce."
rationales.conformity: He experiences guilt for having "broken a rule" after speaking up, because this time he did not simply absorb criticism and preserve the usual pattern.

rationales.achievement: She treats Arjun's enrollment and report cards as a personal performance metric, saying "the only scoreboard I have is my children's report cards."
rationales.power: She pushes the Olympiad course partly because of status comparison, fixating on "Kavitha aunty's son" and wanting the standing that comes from her child's visible success.
rationales.conformity: Family and community expectations drive the decision, with temple and family-gathering comparisons shaping why she "pushed for it."
rationales.benevolence: She enrolls Arjun despite doubts about readiness and notes he will "just absorb it," showing her ambitions are overriding his needs.

rationales.tradition: The entry centers on preserving a family practice, from Amma making pongal "the way she's done it for sixty years" to wanting to learn it until "my hands remember."
rationales.benevolence: She is moved that Amma cooked because "Arjun mentioned he missed her pongal rice," and her response is to stand beside her mother and carry that care forward.

rationales.conformity: She frames the action as straightforward obedience: "Amma asked, I did it," emphasizing compliance with family expectations.
rationales.tradition: Reorganizing the prayer room and placing the "Lakshmi lamp" correctly reflects maintaining established household religious practice.

rationales.conformity: She gives an automatic "no before I even thought about it," enforcing expected behavior rather than engaging her daughter's request.
rationales.tradition: Refusing to let Divya skip the "temple youth group meeting" prioritizes a standing family-religious obligation over a birthday party.
rationales.benevolence: She recognizes Divya has "given up trying to negotiate" but still runs "the same script," showing her daughter's emotional needs are being sidelined.

rationales.achievement: She comes prepared for scrutiny, noting "I had the spreadsheet ready this time" when her budget was questioned.
rationales.tradition: Working on the "Pongal committee meeting" and finalizing festival logistics supports continuity of a cultural celebration.

rationales.achievement: She treats the low score as a performance starting point, insisting "It's one test. Baseline," which keeps the focus on improvement and results.

rationales.security: The entry is driven by concern that Amma "can't be the one who tells" and that they cannot "pretend she doesn't need help," showing attention to household safety and sustainability.
rationales.tradition: She hesitates because Amma's routines, from "morning puja" to arranging spice boxes, feel like an order that should not be casually dismantled.
rationales.benevolence: She is trying to protect Amma's dignity and wellbeing, saying that taking the system apart feels like telling her mother "she's done."

rationales.achievement: She admires that Divya has been "grinding at it for months" and finally played the piece "clean, start to finish," highlighting disciplined accomplishment.
rationales.benevolence: Her focus is on her daughter's joy, from the "real smile" to wanting to tell her she was "radiant" without turning it into another evaluative comment.

rationales.achievement: The moment centers on measurable progress, with Arjun bringing over his "sixty-first percentile" result and her reading it as advancement.
rationales.benevolence: She reflects that she "should have let the moment be what it was" and wanted to affirm him directly, showing concern for his emotional experience rather than only the score.

rationales.self_direction: She is pulled toward choosing her own path, feeling alive at the idea of "joining her school project in Kenya" and rejecting the expectation to just stay put.
rationales.stimulation: She explicitly craves novelty and challenge, saying the Kenya project made her "heart race" and that staying makes her feel like she's "slowly turning to stone."
rationales.security: The stable life of the farmhouse and school does not reassure her; she is willing to unsettle that stability because "the staying" feels deadening.

rationales.self_direction: She acts on her own preference by booking the Nairobi flight and telling the school she's taking a sabbatical to pursue the path she wants.
rationales.stimulation: Booking a preliminary trip to Nairobi to explore a new project shows active pursuit of a fresh, uncertain experience.
rationales.security: She leaves the farmhouse and home logistics unresolved, brushing them off as something to "deal with later," which deprioritizes stability.

rationales.achievement: She puts extra effort into each report because "if I'm going to put my name on something, it should be done properly," emphasizing competence and standards.
rationales.conformity: She carefully calibrates comments to each family so that "no parent should feel embarrassed or caught off guard," showing strong attention to social expectations and smooth interactions.
rationales.benevolence: Her wording is tailored to protect others, such as softening criticism for Mrs. Tan and giving detailed feedback where it will help, which reflects care for families she deals with closely.

rationales.self_direction: She suppresses her own judgment and says "I nodded too" even though she believes the new approach "creates chaos in practice."
rationales.conformity: Her choice is driven by not wanting to be the dissenter, reasoning that "nobody else was going to say anything, so why should I be the one."
rationales.tradition: She defends the existing system because "the current curriculum has worked for fifteen years" and students need "structure and clear expectations."

rationales.self_direction: She acts against her own conviction, admitting "I should have said something" but choosing silence because she did not want the attention and fallout.
rationales.conformity: Avoiding a public confrontation is central to her decision, since speaking up would have made "everyone" look at her and forced others to acknowledge the incident.
rationales.benevolence: She leaves Mr. Goh unsupported after a disrespectful joke and later feels she "failed some kind of test," indicating she did not protect someone close in her work community.

rationales.security: She values the call's stable predictability, describing the repeated exchange as "comfortable. Or at least it's familiar."
rationales.conformity: The interaction is tightly self-monitored and indirect, with both of them "pretend[ing] everything that needs to be said has been said" to keep the peace.
rationales.tradition: Calling "Mother at 7pm as usual" and maintaining the long-standing script reflects a habitual, filial ritual sustained over decades.

rationales.achievement: He defends the quality of the work and his track record, stressing the effort already invested in proposals and that the model is "good work."
rationales.power: He is preoccupied with influence and deference, angry that people did not "honor their word" and that others are now in a decision process he expected to control.
rationales.security: The delayed review and sudden competitors feel destabilizing, and he reacts to that uncertainty as a threat rather than something tolerable.

rationales.achievement: He leaves the meeting "feeling solid about the project" and is already strategizing the next presentation to secure the commission successfully.
rationales.power: He reads the family hierarchy, decides who really controls the choice, and plans to shape the pitch accordingly, showing a strong focus on influence and control.

rationales.hedonism: After an unpleasant family dinner, he turns to immediate sensory comfort by opening "a 2015 Barolo I'd been saving."

rationales.achievement: He frames the opportunity as something he "earned" through years of work and dismisses competing artists as lacking comparable large-scale accomplishments.
rationales.power: The entry is dominated by status and entitlement, from expecting mutual obligations to pay off to rejecting a process where he must compete rather than be deferred to.

rationales.self_direction: He envies Joachim's ability to "just make things" and is troubled by how far he has drifted from making work on his own internal terms.
rationales.power: He describes his current world as constant positioning over "who gets credit, who controls the timeline, who has final say," and the entry treats that status game as hollow rather than satisfying.

rationales.hedonism: He savors the evening's pleasures, from the "grey Zegna" and "Patek" to the satisfaction he feels driving home after the event.
rationales.achievement: Recognition is meaningful because he believes he has "earned that standing through decades of difficult work," tying the moment to demonstrated success.
rationales.power: He explicitly values "the standing" that lets him walk into a room and have others know who he is, showing satisfaction in status and influence.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects managerial demands and immerses himself in solving the sculptural problem directly, emphasizing "just me and the clay."
rationales.hedonism: The day is guided by intrinsic enjoyment, from feeling "better than I have in weeks" to ending with a good Riesling while looking at what he made.
rationales.power: He values the absence of status performance, saying there was "no audience" and "nothing to prove to anyone," which runs against seeking recognition and standing.

rationales.self_direction: He tells the clients "the truth" even though it may cost him money, choosing the work he actually believes in over the more lucrative option.
rationales.achievement: He insists that the smaller proposal is "better art," showing commitment to artistic excellence rather than merely maximizing the contract.
rationales.power: He explicitly turns away from the bigger, more status-signaling commission and says "the status game has diminishing returns," rejecting prestige as the main guide.
rationales.universalism: He favors the piece that "felt inevitable" and "belonged to the land rather than being imposed on it," showing care for fitting the work to a broader environment rather than dominating it.

rationales.achievement: Her focus on the spelling test score, putting the paper on the fridge, and dwelling on the comparison to "full marks" treats performance as meaningful and worth tracking.
rationales.conformity: She "just changed the subject to the soup being too salty" instead of confronting her mother-in-law, prioritizing social smoothness over direct objection.
rationales.tradition: She frames the harsh comparison as something her mother-in-law does because "she cares," showing acceptance of an older family style of evaluation rather than rejecting it.
rationales.benevolence: She told Jia Hui she was proud and was pained when she "watched Jia Hui's face fall," showing care for her daughter's feelings and wellbeing.

rationales.achievement: She calls the enrichment center "an investment" and says she wants proof through "full marks," tying the decision to visible performance and success.
rationales.security: Paying "$800 a month" while repeatedly opening the banking app shows she is straining financial comfort and stability in pursuit of results.
rationales.benevolence: She makes the expensive registration because she wants to "do right by her" daughter, centering her child's future over her own comfort.

rationales.self_direction: Her thinking is driven by what "other mothers are doing" and by external milestones like "PSLE," suggesting she is letting outside standards dictate her choices.
rationales.hedonism: She is "running on four hours of sleep," shaking from coffee, and still piling on tasks, clearly sacrificing rest and immediate wellbeing.
rationales.achievement: She interprets "behind" as failure and worries about her child "playing catch-up," showing a strong fixation on performance and benchmarks.
rationales.conformity: The pressure of "every other parent" using multiple tuition classes pushes her toward prevailing parental norms rather than her own independent judgment.
rationales.benevolence: She stays up at 2am comforting Ming and keeps pushing through exhaustion to care for both children, putting their needs ahead of herself.

rationales.achievement: She keeps a notebook "for tracking both kids' progress" and immediately orders comprehension workbooks, treating improvement as something to actively manage.
rationales.security: She lists the bonus, maxed CPF contributions, and "eight months of expenses" while still planning for job loss or medical costs, showing strong attention to preparedness and stability.
rationales.conformity: She lies that they are still "exploring options" because she is sensitive to other parents' judgments and wants to avoid social disapproval.
rationales.tradition: Her mother's saying, "save for nine winters," is invoked as wisdom she now embraces, showing deference to inherited family guidance.
rationales.benevolence: Her detailed tracking, briefing her husband, and buying materials are all aimed at supporting Jia Hui's development and protecting the family.

rationales.achievement: She criticizes the adults' focus on "schools," "scholarships," and "percentile rankings," then regrets that atmosphere crowded out her daughter's childhood.
rationales.conformity: Her discomfort with the cousins "comparing" status markers shows resistance to the family norm of competitive child-evaluation.
rationales.benevolence: Saying "I should have let her be a child" reflects concern that Jia Hui's emotional needs mattered more than the adults' prestige talk.

rationales.achievement: She insists on more workbooks and says her daughter needs "to not struggle later," framing parenting around readiness and performance.
rationales.security: Her repeated emphasis on being "prepared" and "ready for the real world" shows a strong focus on preventing future risk.
rationales.conformity: The parent WhatsApp groups and "how the other kids are doing" shape her judgment, indicating that peer norms heavily influence her choices.
rationales.benevolence: Even after snapping, she is acting from daily involvement and a wish to make sure her daughter has what she needs.

rationales.self_direction: She starts questioning the KPI mindset she "brought home" and recognizes her daughter is "not a project," showing a move toward choosing her own way rather than inherited metrics.
rationales.hedonism: She says yes to "just play," builds a blanket fort, reads together, and experiences two hours of shared joy for its own sake.
rationales.achievement: She explicitly rejects "metrics, targets, quarterly reviews" and says "maybe it's not about measuring at all," pushing back against performance-driven thinking.
rationales.conformity: By stepping away from "developmental benchmarks" and "comparison tables," she resists the social pressure to evaluate her child by standard competitive measures.
rationales.benevolence: Her realization that her daughter "just wants her mother to be there" and her choice to be fully present center the child's emotional needs.

rationales.achievement: He had "the data" and had prepared for the meeting since Thursday, showing investment in performing competently and having his work land.
rationales.power: He goes quiet after being talked over and admits saying nothing made him look "weaker," so the session is defined by lost influence and status in the room.
rationales.conformity: He holds back because he worries he would look like "the one making a scene," restraining himself to avoid violating meeting norms.

rationales.achievement: He has already "run the numbers" and is focused on removing an efficiency bottleneck for his team, showing task competence and results orientation.
rationales.power: What gets to him is that "it's Dave's signature on the line," meaning his plans are blocked by someone else's control and authority.

rationales.achievement: He brought "solid margins data" and a vendor swap proposal, indicating a clear effort to improve performance and outcomes.
rationales.power: When Karen shuts him down publicly, he "folded" and gave up the push, marking a loss of influence and control in the meeting.
rationales.conformity: He accepts "that's not how we onboard vendors here" and stops pressing, yielding to established process and group expectations.

rationales.achievement: He is fixated on the fact that they are paying "twelve percent more than we need to" and wants to keep pushing a cost-saving change.
rationales.power: He plans to "bring it up again differently" and repackage it so it lands, showing active effort to shape decisions and regain influence.

rationales.achievement: He is energized by "second highest in the region, up four percent" and by having "numbers" and a "track record," showing strong attachment to measurable success.
rationales.power: He immediately agrees to present at the leadership summit because he wants "them to see what my team has built," signaling comfort with visibility, influence, and status.
His frustration centers on Derek bypassing him and not respecting "the hierarchy," which leaves him feeling his authority is being undercut.
rationales.conformity: He lets Derek finish because he "didn't want to embarrass him in front of everyone," prioritizing social restraint and smoothness over direct correction.
